Additionally, poor mental health heightens sensitivity to hormonal fluctuations, creating an interaction effect where psychological distress exacerbates the adverse impacts of job stressors on menstrual symptoms. The findings underscore that interventions addressing workplace stress and mental health support may be particularly effective in reducing menstruation-related symptoms among female workers, as coworker support showed stronger associations with mental health outcomes than job demands alone.

title: Fear of Disease Progression in Patients with Type 2 Diabetes Construction and Validation of a Nomogram Prediction Model
summary: Patients with type 2 diabetes mellitus frequently experience fear of disease progression (FDP), a psychological condition that significantly impairs blood glucose control, treatment compliance, and overall physical function. This study developed and validated a predictive nomogram identifying key risk factors for FDP, which include female gender, rural residence, increased disease complications and comorbidities, lower family resilience, and poorer illness perception. Socioeconomic factors such as lower household income, unemployment, and unfavorable medical expense payment methods also contribute to elevated FDP risk, alongside longer disease duration and lower health literacy. The findings demonstrate that FDP creates a bidirectional negative cycle, simultaneously worsening metabolic control while adversely affecting mental health and disease outcomes. These results underscore the importance of screening for psychological distress in diabetes patients, particularly among vulnerable populations including rural residents and women. The validated nomogram provides clinicians with a tool to identify high-risk patients requiring targeted psychosocial interventions to improve treatment adherence and clinical outcomes.

title: A Cohort Study on Factors Affecting Sleep Quality in Women Undergoing Intrauterine Sperm Insemination (IUI) Treatment
summary: This cohort study demonstrates that sleep quality is significantly compromised in women undergoing intrauterine insemination (IUI) treatment, with approximately 30.5% experiencing sleep disorders. The impaired sleep results from infertility-related stress acting through the hypothalamic-pituitary axis, combined with hormonal fluctuations triggered by assisted reproductive treatments that lead to physical complaints and psychological distress. Poor sleep quality creates a bidirectional relationship with anxiety and depression, both of which are elevated in infertile women and further exacerbated by specific infertility causes such as ovulation disorders and male factor infertility. Notably, poor sleep quality is associated with reduced reproductive outcomes including fewer retrieved oocytes, lower embryo quality, and decreased fertilization rates. Sleep disorders are also linked to broader health complications including hypertension, diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular events, as well as reproductive hormonal disruptions. The findings underscore that addressing sleep quality alongside psychological support may be essential for optimizing treatment outcomes in women undergoing fertility interventions, particularly those with endometriosis and ovulatory dysfunction.

title: Latent profiles of post-traumatic growth in patients with recent hysterectomy: psychosocial predictors and stigma-associated outcomes
summary:
# Summary

This study examined post-traumatic growth patterns in women undergoing hysterectomy for endometriosis and related gynecological conditions, identifying critical psychosocial factors influencing psychological outcomes. Hysterectomy causes multiple adverse physiological consequences including pelvic floor dysfunction, urinary and sexual dysfunction, and decreased estrogen and progesterone secretion, with the latter directly enhancing negative emotions and increasing risk for anxiety and depression. The research revealed that post-traumatic growth substantially mitigates post-traumatic stress symptoms and psychological distress while promoting positive psychological well-being; however, stigma—encompassing internalized shame and negative self-evaluation—significantly inhibits this growth and perpetuates psychological distress. Patients with greater post-traumatic growth showed better mental health outcomes and were more likely to be middle-aged (40–59 years), have higher education levels, and report lower stigma scores, whereas those with high internalized shame and social isolation experienced negative growth trajectories. These findings indicate that interventions targeting stigma reduction and promoting post-traumatic growth may be essential to counteract the substantial mental and physical health burdens associated with hysterectomy in endometriosis patients.

title: Factors affecting healthcare access for dysmenorrhoea: a scoping review protocol
summary: Dysmenorrhoea, characterized by moderate to severe pelvic and abdominal pain during menstruation, frequently occurs with associated symptoms including back pain and vomiting, and substantially impacts mental health, quality of life, and productivity through workplace and educational absenteeism. Secondary dysmenorrhoea can signal underlying gynaecological pathologies such as endometriosis, fibroids, adenomyosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease, yet diagnostic delays spanning years are common, leaving many cases undiagnosed. Healthcare access for dysmenorrhoea is hindered by multiple interconnected barriers including low menstrual health literacy, pain invalidation by healthcare providers, menstrual stigma, socioeconomic constraints, and inadequate social support. Evidence-based management improves with increased menstrual health literacy and consultation with healthcare practitioners, yet individual beliefs about illness, health system limitations, and broader sociocultural factors create complex obstacles to appropriate care. This scoping review protocol aims to comprehensively examine the multifactorial factors affecting dysmenorrhoea healthcare access, recognizing that successful management requires addressing not only clinical symptoms but also psychosocial, economic, and systemic dimensions of the healthcare experience.

title: A meta-analysis of the correlation between the duration of the second stage of labor and recent postpartum pelvic floor injury in primiparous
summary: This meta-analysis examines how prolonged second-stage labor affects postpartum pelvic floor injury in first-time mothers. Prolonged labor causes significant mechanical stress on pelvic floor muscles, fascia, and ligaments through sustained compression and stretching, leading to tissue damage, reduced elasticity, and nerve dysfunction. The study found that extended second-stage labor substantially increases the risk of urinary incontinence (OR=1.95), pelvic floor pain (OR=1.11), and urinary retention (OR=2.40), while showing no significant association with pelvic organ prolapse, fecal incontinence, or sexual dysfunction. The pathophysiological mechanisms involve muscle fatigue and strength decline from prolonged pressure, lactic acid accumulation causing local ischemia and hypoxia, and fascia tearing with inflammatory responses that stimulate pain signaling. The study recommends preventive and therapeutic interventions including pelvic floor muscle training to strengthen the urethral sphincter, thermal therapy to improve blood circulation and relieve muscle tension, and electrical stimulation to restore nerve conduction function in affected women.

title: Experiences of menstrual health and amenorrhoea in eating disorder inpatient units in England: a subgroup analysis from a lived experience led, qualitative study
summary: This qualitative study examines how eating disorder inpatient units in England manage menstrual health, revealing significant gaps between clinical practice and patient needs. Eating disorders—particularly anorexia nervosa, bulimia, and binge-eating disorder—disrupt menstruation through multiple pathways, including weight loss, psychological stress, and excessive exercise, which inhibit the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis and trigger functional hypothalamic amenorrhea. While treatment services closely monitor menstruation as a biomarker of recovery and use its resumption to guide discharge planning and treatment outcome assessment, the study documents that units fail to provide adequate menstrual health information, menstrual products, or emotional support around menstruation-related symptoms such as nausea and pain. Notably, patients reported forced disclosure of menstrual products, shame surrounding menstruation, and difficulty distinguishing between eating disorder symptoms and other menstrual conditions like endometriosis, which went undiagnosed due to insufficient education. The study highlights a critical paradox: while menstrual resumption is heavily emphasized as a health indicator and recovery marker, eating disorder services overlook the practical, physical, and psychological support necessary for healthy menstrual functioning and the complex relationships between menstruation, body weight perception, and eating disorder relapse risk.

title: Group-Based Integrative Pain Management: Feasibility of a Factorial Randomized Trial in Safety-Net Primary Care
journal: Journal of Primary Care & Community Health
summary:
# Summary

This factorial randomized trial demonstrated that group-based integrative pain management interventions—specifically group acupuncture and integrative group medical visits (IGMVs)—effectively reduce chronic pain and its associated burden in safety-net primary care settings. Both interventions significantly decreased pain impact scores, pain interference, and depression and anxiety symptoms while improving global physical health, physical function, and self-efficacy. Beyond individual pain outcomes, IGMVs additionally fostered interpersonal benefits including increased social support, reduced social isolation, and diminished pain-related stigma, which are critical given that social determinants such as stigma, racism, and isolation substantially contribute to poor pain outcomes and opioid overuse. The study found that 86% of participants receiving study interventions reported clinically relevant pain improvements compared to only 20% in control groups, while also reducing healthcare utilization and opioid use. These group-based approaches address systemic barriers by providing extended visit time, improving access to multimodal care, and lowering costs, making them particularly valuable for underserved populations managing chronic pain.

title: ‘The Difficulty of Diagnosis Compromises Patient Care for People With Endometriosis’: Interview Study With Aotearoa New Zealand General Practitioners
journal: Health Expectations : An International Journal of Public Participation in Health Care and Health Policy
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affects approximately 10% of women and people presumed female at birth, yet diagnostic difficulty and heterogeneous symptom presentation significantly compromise patient care in Aotearoa-New Zealand. General practitioners identified that patients experience long, difficult diagnostic journeys characterized by frequent misdiagnosis, dismissal, and traumatic care experiences, with impacts spanning work, education, relationships, mental health, and fertility. Systemic barriers including overloaded specialist appointments, resource shortages in the public health system, short consultation times, cultural taboos around menstruation, and financial barriers to both treatment and specialist access—particularly pronounced in regional areas—create a "postcode lottery" in endometriosis care. GP validation of patient experiences improves therapeutic alliances, whereas specialist dismissal and poor treatment lead patients to minimize their symptom presentation, creating hidden morbidity in mental health outcomes. Solutions identified by GPs include expanded endometriosis guidelines, specialist GP or nurse training, improved patient relationship-building, and substantial public health system investment to address access inequities and resource constraints.

title: Associations of adolescent menstrual symptoms with school absences and educational attainment: analysis of a prospective cohort study
summary: This prospective cohort study demonstrates that adolescent menstrual symptoms significantly impair educational outcomes through multiple pathways. Heavy or prolonged bleeding and severe menstrual pain are both strongly associated with increased school absences (16.6% and 12.8% more time off, respectively) and persistent absence patterns, ultimately resulting in lower GCSE examination scores and reduced likelihood of achieving top grades. Beyond simple absenteeism, menstrual symptoms impair academic performance through concentration difficulties, fatigue (potentially exacerbated by iron deficiency anemia from heavy bleeding), and anxiety about symptom management at school. The study identifies underlying gynecological conditions—including endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, and fibroids—as potential contributors to severe menstrual symptoms, while also highlighting structural barriers such as inadequate period product provision and restricted toilet access that compound educational disadvantage. Oral contraceptive use shows promise in reducing menstrual pain severity, suggesting a potential intervention pathway. These findings underscore that menstrual health is fundamentally linked to educational equity in adolescents and warrants clinical and policy attention to reduce both symptom burden and school-based barriers to symptom management.

title: Sexual distress with partnered face-to-face sexual activity: an exploratory qualitative study with heterosexual cis people who seek and do not seek professional help
summary: This qualitative study identifies sexual distress in partnered face-to-face sexual activity as a multifactorial phenomenon fundamentally intertwined with both mental health and relationship quality, with sexual distress serving as a diagnostic criterion for sexual dysfunction. The research reveals that depression, worry, emotion regulation difficulties, and repetitive negative thinking—transdiagnostic psychological factors—significantly contribute to sexual distress, which is also shaped by cognitive, emotional, and communication factors within relationships. Gender differences emerged, with women predominantly reporting sexual pain and difficulties with sexual pleasure, while men emphasized desire discrepancies and erectile disorder; notably, those seeking professional help highlighted concerns about genital function and negative emotions. The study demonstrates that sexual distress related to sexual function operates within a broader internalizing spectrum of psychopathology and is influenced by internalized sexual stigma and media pressure alongside individual psychological characteristics and couple-level communication dynamics. These findings underscore that effective interventions must address not only sexual-specific factors but also transdiagnostic psychological processes and couple communication patterns to holistically treat sexual distress in partnered contexts.

title: Disclosing Inflammatory Bowel Disease: A Systematic Review and Meta-Synthesis Exploring the Experience of, and Barriers and Facilitators to, Self-Disclosure
journal: Journal of Clinical Psychology in Medical Settings
summary:
# Summary

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD), which encompasses Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, is a progressive autoimmune condition that produces significant physical symptoms including abdominal pain, diarrhea, fatigue, and weight loss. Beyond these direct physiological effects, patients with IBD face substantial psychological burden stemming from disease-related stigmatization, which drives psychological distress, reduced health-related quality of life, decreased self-esteem, and diminished medication adherence. The introduction of biologic therapies such as infliximab in 1999 substantially improved disease activity control and reduced treatment-associated side effects, thereby enhancing outcomes for IBD patients. Despite pharmacological advances, however, stigma remains a critical barrier to patient wellbeing, underscoring the importance of addressing psychological and social dimensions alongside medical management. This systematic review emphasizes that self-disclosure decisions among IBD patients reflect complex negotiations between symptom burden, fear of social judgment, and therapeutic needs, highlighting the interconnection between physical disease manifestations and mental health outcomes in chronic inflammatory conditions.

title: Enhancing Effect of eHealth Use on the Associations Between Social Supports and Well-Being in Japanese Employed Women Providing Childcare or Care: Bayesian Structural Equation Modeling Study
summary: This study demonstrates that eHealth use significantly enhances the relationship between social support and well-being in employed Japanese women managing childcare or elder care responsibilities. Social support—traditionally from family and friends—directly improves well-being, life satisfaction, and happiness while reducing anxiety and loneliness, with standardized effects of 0.20 (95% CI 0.13-0.27) in participants without eHealth access. Internet-based resources amplify these protective effects by providing immediate access to support networks, reducing social isolation, and improving perceived responsiveness and emotional resilience. The research highlights how employment, while beneficial for well-being, creates a "double burden" through work-family conflict that reduces overall well-being; eHealth interventions help mitigate this negative impact by strengthening social connections. The findings suggest that digital health platforms serve as critical moderators that enhance both the perception of social support and downstream improvements in mental health outcomes, particularly for women navigating multiple caregiving roles.

title: Comparing the Effectiveness of Expressive Writing and Relaxation on Mental Health and Treatment Success in Women Undergoing Assisted Reproductive Technology: A Randomized Controlled Trial
summary:
# Summary

This randomized controlled trial evaluated psychological interventions for women undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART), examining how infertility-related mental health challenges impact treatment outcomes. Infertility and ART procedures trigger substantial psychological burden, causing depression, anxiety, and stress that can paradoxically reduce treatment success rates. The study compared expressive writing and Benson relaxation interventions, finding that expressive writing significantly reduced depression and mental distress within the treatment group, while relaxation techniques similarly decreased stress, anxiety, and depression through physiological and psychological mechanisms. However, neither intervention demonstrated significant improvements in pregnancy rates or treatment success outcomes. The research highlights that while psychological interventions effectively ameliorate the emotional burden of infertility treatment, additional support strategies may be necessary to translate mental health improvements into enhanced clinical fertility outcomes.

title: The Relationship Between Illness Uncertainty and Social Support Among Cancer Patients
summary: This meta-analytic study examined the relationship between illness uncertainty and social support among cancer patients, revealing a moderate negative correlation where stronger social support reduces illness uncertainty and associated psychological distress. The strength of this association varied significantly by cancer type, with patients with ovarian and lung cancer experiencing higher illness uncertainty compared to those with bladder cancer, while developed countries showed stronger correlations than developing nations. Social support functions as a protective factor by mitigating illness uncertainty and its downstream effects on quality of life and treatment prognosis. Cancer treatment commonly precipitates multiple burdens including fatigue, pain, and depression, which are compounded in high-mortality cancers where disease threat intensifies both psychological distress and illness uncertainty. The Multidimensional Scale of Perceived Social Support (MSPSS) was the most effective measurement tool for assessing social support in these populations. These findings underscore the critical importance of enhancing social support interventions as a means to reduce illness uncertainty and improve psychological outcomes across diverse cancer populations.

title: Assessment of Mental and Chronic Health Conditions as Determinants of Health Care Needs and Digital Innovations for Women With Sexual Dysfunction: Cross-Sectional Population-Based Survey Study in Germany
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional German population study demonstrates that chronic health conditions significantly impair sexual function in women, with 75.2% of women with chronic conditions experiencing sexual dysfunction compared to 62.5% without such conditions. Mental health conditions and cancer emerge as the strongest predictors of sexual dysfunction, with major depression associated with sexual dysfunction rates ranging from 45-95%, while gynecological conditions, diabetes, inflammatory bowel disease, and cancer also show strong associations. The underlying mechanisms are multifaceted, involving somatic changes such as altered blood flow, pelvic floor dysfunction, hormonal imbalances, and neurobiological factors, as well as psychological symptoms like depression-related loss of drive and reduced attention. Sexual dysfunction significantly impacts quality of life and relationships, with being in a partnership serving as a protective factor against clinically relevant sexual distress. Digital health solutions, including online information and treatment services, show promise in improving sexual function and reducing sexual distress, potentially addressing the substantial gap in diagnosis and treatment access for women experiencing these interconnected health challenges.

title: Ethical analysis of the change of values in healthcare
summary:
# Summary

This ethical analysis examines how shifts in healthcare values away from beneficence toward economic priorities generate significant moral distress among healthcare professionals, with particular impact on vulnerable patient populations including those with endometriosis. The research reveals a cascade of psychological and occupational consequences: moral dissonance between practitioners' values and required behaviors leads to moral distress, which progresses to moral injury, compassion fatigue, and ultimately workforce drain and burnout. Low-value care—practices unlikely to benefit patients—disproportionately affects vulnerable groups and violates beneficence and patient autonomy, exacerbated by systemic time shortages that prevent adherence to best clinical practices. Interventions addressing only moral distress at the individual level prove insufficient for improving patient well-being, whereas structural healthcare reforms coupled with virtue ethics cultivation—including resilience, moral courage, and empathy—alongside practices like shared decision-making, value reflection, and mindfulness training demonstrate greater efficacy in preventing moral injury and improving both healthcare worker well-being and care quality.

title: Natural Tooth Count and Sleep Quality in Older Adults: The Mediating Effect of Temporomandibular Joint Pain
summary: This study demonstrates that natural tooth count significantly influences sleep quality in older adults, with temporomandibular joint (TMJ) pain serving as a key mediating mechanism. Individuals with fewer natural teeth experience altered jaw biomechanics and increased TMJ pain, which in turn disrupts sleep patterns and reduces sleep quality and duration. The relationship operates through multiple pathways: tooth loss directly impairs masticatory efficiency and alters sensory input to the nervous system, while also triggering social discomfort that elevates stress levels—all factors that compromise sleep. Additionally, TMJ pain is independently associated with worse sleep outcomes, and this association is partially mediated by the effects of reduced tooth count on jaw function. The findings suggest that oral health maintenance, particularly preservation of natural teeth, represents an important but often overlooked factor in promoting healthy sleep in aging populations. Interventions targeting oral health and TMJ dysfunction management may therefore offer therapeutic benefits for sleep quality in older adults.

title: Socio-psychological distress, violence, and food insecurity in women undergoing gynecological examinations: insights from a cross-sectional study of an Italian Tertiary Clinic
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study of women attending an Italian tertiary gynecological clinic identified multiple interconnected risk factors for socio-psychological distress, violence, and food insecurity. Violence emerged as the strongest predictor of psychological distress (OR: 4.65), followed by economic difficulties (OR: 3.91) and oncological conditions (OR: 3.76), while chronic gynecological conditions including endometriosis independently contributed to psychological distress. Benign gynecological conditions, alcohol use, economic difficulties, and food insecurity were all significant risk factors for experiencing violence, with economic hardship serving as a central driver linking all three outcomes. Women experiencing distress, violence, or food insecurity reported substantially worse self-reported health status and were more likely to be unemployed and unmarried, highlighting the interconnected nature of socioeconomic vulnerability, interpersonal trauma, and gynecological health in this population.

title: Mapping the health outcomes of menstrual inequity: a comprehensive scoping review
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review comprehensively documents the health outcomes associated with menstrual inequity, revealing substantial impacts on both physical and mental health. Menstrual inequity—encompassing poverty, lack of access to products and facilities, stigma, and insufficient education—is strongly associated with infectious complications including urinary tract and reproductive tract infections, as well as severe mental health consequences including anxiety, depression, and general distress. Specific menstrual conditions such as dysmenorrhea, endometriosis, heavy bleeding, and irregular menstruation are linked to reduced quality of life and mental health diagnoses, while normalization of these conditions paradoxically prevents individuals from seeking healthcare. The review identifies multiple interconnected pathways through which menstrual inequity undermines wellbeing: limited access to menstrual management resources directly increases infection risk, while menstrual stigma, discrimination, and lack of education contribute to psychological stress and negative emotional outcomes. These findings emphasize that menstrual health is not merely a reproductive issue but a fundamental determinant of women's overall health equity and quality of life.

title: Empowering Women Through Knowledge: A Systematic Review of Literature on Menstrual and Reproductive Health Literacy
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review examines menstrual and reproductive health literacy as a critical determinant of women's overall well-being and personal empowerment. The study reveals significant knowledge gaps across populations, particularly among adolescents, with substantial deficits in understanding basic reproductive physiology—such as menstrual cycle length (58.9% correct knowledge) and ovulation timing (46.3% correct knowledge of ovum lifespan). While women demonstrate relatively strong knowledge of basic definitions (93% correctly identify menstruation; 75.3% understand ovulation), understanding of menstrual abnormalities and their causes remains limited, with only 33% recognizing that bleeding longer than seven days is excessive and knowledge of dysmenorrhea triggers ranging from 47% to 61%. School-based interventions are identified as promising approaches to improve reproductive health literacy, with comprehensive education addressing anatomical structures (ovaries, fallopian tubes, uterus), hormonal regulation (estrogen and progesterone involvement), and physiological factors affecting reproductive health (stress, exercise, diet, and lifestyle factors). The review underscores that menstrual health literacy directly influences women's access to and experience of reproductive healthcare, highlighting the need for targeted educational interventions to empower women through knowledge of their reproductive biology and health maintenance.

title: Fuelling Recovery: Is There a Role for Radiation Therapists in Optimising Nutrition for Women With Breast Cancer?
summary: Women with breast cancer undergoing radiation therapy face multiple nutritional challenges stemming from both treatment side effects and pre-existing dietary habits. Radiation therapy directly causes gastrointestinal complications including nausea, vomiting, and dysphagia, which collectively lead to severe nutritional deficiency and weight loss requiring potential hospitalization. Additionally, most women in this population fail to meet dietary guidelines, consuming inadequate fruits and vegetables while exhibiting elevated rates of overweight and obesity, compounded by high prevalence of comorbidities including hypertension, high cholesterol, anxiety, and depression—all conditions associated with poor diet quality. Despite eighty percent of women expressing interest in nutritional interventions and radiation therapists being ideally positioned for regular patient contact, significant gaps exist in healthcare training, with radiation oncology practitioners reporting low confidence in providing dietary advice and acknowledging knowledge as a barrier to diet promotion. The article emphasizes that structured nutritional support can reduce weight loss and improve quality of life, suggesting radiation therapists could play an expanded role in optimizing nutrition through specialized training and systematic dietary assessment protocols. Addressing these unmet nutritional supportive care needs represents a critical opportunity to enhance cancer recovery outcomes in this vulnerable population.

title: The effects of exercise frequency on sexual function and psychological health in infertile women
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how exercise frequency influences sexual function and psychological health in infertile women. Infertility was strongly associated with sexual dysfunction, anxiety, and depression, with longer infertility duration exacerbating these effects through both psychological and physiological mechanisms. Regular physical activity (≥once weekly) significantly reduced anxiety and depression while improving sexual function scores across all domains—desire, arousal, satisfaction, and orgasm—compared to sedentary women. Women engaging in no regular exercise exhibited the highest depression scores, highest sexual dysfunction prevalence (23.17%), and lowest marital satisfaction, suggesting that exercise benefits sexual and relational health through multiple pathways including improved cardiovascular function, enhanced genital blood flow, hormone modulation, and reduced systemic inflammation. The study demonstrates a bidirectional relationship wherein psychological distress impairs sexual response and motivation for physical activity, while exercise interrupts this negative cycle. These findings support exercise as an evidence-based intervention addressing both the psychological burden and sexual health complications that commonly accompany infertility.

title: Finite element modeling in obstetrics and gynecology: advances, applications, and challenges
summary: Finite element modeling (FEM) has emerged as a powerful computational tool for understanding complex pelvic floor dysfunction in obstetrics and gynecology, enabling detailed mechanical analysis of structures including the levator ani muscle, urethra, bladder, and cervix. The research demonstrates that multiple biomechanical factors contribute to pelvic pathology: obesity, age, parity, and levator ani muscle injury collectively compromise pelvic organ support and increase prolapse risk, while levator ani muscle damage increases hiatal opening especially under elevated intra-abdominal pressure. For urinary continence, the studies reveal that levator ani muscle activity is essential for urethra function, and that stress urinary incontinence results from complex interactions between increased bladder tissue stiffness, greater bladder neck displacement, and asymmetric pelvic deformation. Surgical interventions analyzed through FEM show that continuous stitch mesh fixation effectively reduces uterine and vaginal wall displacement, though increasing stitch number provides no additional benefit, while sacrospinous fixation offers an alternative approach to reduce organ displacement. The modeling approach also identified abnormal mechanical and biophysical properties associated with endometriosis and revealed that cervical insufficiency involves softened tissue and shortened cervical length, which cause widespread stress distribution and increased deformation susceptibility. These computational findings provide mechanistic insights that could guide clinical management strategies for pelvic floor disorders and pregnancy complications.

title: Policy solutions to improve access to fertility treatment and optimise patient care: consensus from an expert forum
summary: This expert consensus article addresses the significant mental health burden and access barriers in fertility treatment, particularly for patients with endometriosis and other fertility-threatening conditions. Infertility is strongly associated with depression and anxiety, while involuntary childlessness causes additional psychological harm including ostracism and reduced self-esteem. Endometriosis ranks among the most common causes of female infertility, alongside ovulation disorders, poor egg quality, and fallopian tube damage from untreated infections. The article highlights that maternal age substantially reduces pregnancy success rates with assisted reproductive technology and increases miscarriage risk, making fertility preservation through egg freezing—enhanced by vitrification techniques—increasingly important for patients facing fertility threats from cancer treatment or severe endometriosis. Major barriers to fertility care access include insufficient disease recognition, inadequate financial coverage, limited psychosocial support, and cultural factors, which the experts argue perpetuate health inequities and underscore the need for comprehensive policy reform to optimize patient outcomes and mental health.

title: Investigating Causal Links Between Uterine Prolapse, Urinary Tract Infections, and Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms: A Two-Sample Mendelian Randomization Study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study investigates the causal relationships between uterine prolapse (UP), urinary tract infections (UTIs), and lower urinary tract symptoms (LUTS). The analysis reveals that uterine prolapse has a causal link to increased UTI risk, likely mediated through incomplete bladder emptying, urine stagnation, and urethral obstruction that facilitate bacterial colonization. Multiple mechanisms explain how prolapse generates urinary symptoms: physical obstruction of bladder outflow, activation of detrusor muscle sensory receptors triggering urgency and frequency, and urethral kinking or traction. The study identifies that defective pelvic support—arising from hormone-related atrophy, neurogenic disorders, and metabolic factors—underlies the association between prolapse and multiple urinary complications including UTIs, incontinence, and urinary frequency. Non-surgical interventions including pelvic floor physical therapy and pessaries effectively alleviate UP-related symptoms, while surgical and conservative management of pelvic organ prolapse provides relief from overactive bladder syndrome. These findings emphasize the critical role of pelvic floor muscle strength in maintaining urinary continence and supporting overall pelvic function.

title: The Impact of Gynecological Pathologies on Patients’ Quality of Life from Menarche to Menopause—Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Gynecological pathologies, particularly endometriosis and dysmenorrhea, significantly impair quality of life across reproductive years by causing chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, infertility, and associated psychological distress including anxiety and depression. Dysmenorrhea reduces quality of life by disrupting daily activities and professional performance, while endometriosis causes multiple debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and dyschezia that collectively impact physical, social, and psychological well-being. Endometriosis-related infertility compounds psychological burden, as infertile women experience 60% higher rates of psychological distress, anxiety, and depression alongside marital and sexual difficulties. Both hormonal and surgical treatments effectively reduce endometriosis symptoms and improve quality of life, while lifestyle modifications including physical exercise, smoking cessation, and increased omega-3 fatty acid intake offer additional benefits. The impacts of gynecological conditions extend beyond reproductive years, with menopause causing estrogen-deficient symptoms including anxiety, depression, sleep disturbances, and sexual dysfunction that similarly compromise quality of life.

title: Randomised controlled trial to measure effectiveness and cost-effectiveness of a digital social intervention promoted by primary care clinicians to adults with asthma to improve asthma control: protocol
summary: This randomized controlled trial protocol evaluates a digital social intervention delivered through primary care to improve asthma control in adults. The study addresses the established pathway where uncontrolled asthma leads to anxiety, social isolation, and reduced quality of life, which in turn diminish self-efficacy and asthma self-management capacity. The intervention leverages online health communities to engage patients with peers facing similar conditions, thereby improving self-efficacy, illness understanding, and self-management skills—factors known to enhance asthma control and reduce morbidity. Primary care clinician referral is incorporated as a strategy to optimize patient retention in the digital intervention. The research specifically tests the Asthma+Lung UK online health community platform, which provides asynchronous peer support to break the cycle of psychological effects and loneliness that perpetuate poor disease control. This protocol represents an approach to addressing both the clinical and psychosocial dimensions of uncontrolled asthma through scalable digital infrastructure.

title: Treatment experience of people living with HIV who transitioned from oral to long-acting injectable antiretrovirals in Puerto Rico: A qualitative study
summary: This qualitative study examined the treatment experiences of people living with HIV in Puerto Rico who transitioned from daily oral antiretroviral therapy (ART) to long-acting injectable cabotegravir/rilpivirine (LAI-CAB/RPV). The switch to bimonthly injections significantly improved quality of life and treatment satisfaction by reducing daily medication burden, eliminating the need for daily pill reminders, and minimizing stigma associated with visible medication intake. LAI-CAB/RPV maintained viral suppression comparable to oral ART while improving adherence, particularly by addressing barriers such as complex pill regimens, side effects, and stigma that previously undermined adherence. Participants reported enhanced emotional well-being and more spontaneous social interactions, though some experienced mild injection site discomfort, body aches, or chills that resolved quickly. The study highlights that reducing daily medication frequency substantially improves mental health outcomes and reduces stress related to living with HIV, establishing long-acting injectables as a transformative treatment option that addresses both clinical efficacy and psychosocial needs in HIV management.

title: The 2025 Menopausal Hormone Therapy Guidelines - Korean Society of Menopause
summary: The 2025 Korean Society of Menopause guidelines comprehensively address menopausal hormone therapy and non-hormonal alternatives for managing the complex symptoms arising from estrogen deficiency during menopause. Estrogen deficiency triggers vasomotor symptoms, urogenital atrophy, and bone loss, with menopausal hormone therapy remaining the most effective intervention, achieving approximately 75% symptom reduction for hot flushes and night sweats, while emerging neurokinin receptor antagonists (fezolinetant and elinzanetant) and non-hormonal alternatives including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors, and gabapentin offer viable options for patients with contraindications. Beyond vasomotor symptom relief, MHT improves sleep quality, reduces cardiovascular and metabolic disease risk, and transdermal estrogen therapy demonstrates particular efficacy within three months. For genitourinary syndrome of menopause—a major contributor to sexual dysfunction affecting 85-93% of women through vaginal dryness and dyspareunia—local estrogen therapy, vaginal DHEA, and ospemifene provide effective treatment by restoring vaginal pH and lactobacilli populations. Notably, tibolone demonstrates superior benefits for sexual function including desire, arousal, and lubrication compared to conventional estrogen-progestogen therapy, while psychological interventions like cognitive behavioral therapy complement pharmacological approaches in managing menopausal symptoms and improving overall quality of life.

title: Premenstrual Syndrome-Impact Questionnaire: Cross-cultural adaptation, reliability, and validity of the Turkish version
summary: Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) is a multifactorial condition associated with hormonal changes, neurotransmitter imbalances, and altered prostaglandin levels that manifest as both emotional and physical symptoms. Emotional manifestations include mood swings, depression, anger, and irritability, while physical symptoms encompass weight gain, abdominal cramps, acne, fatigue, breast tenderness, and bloating. PMS is significantly associated with psychological issues and can progress to premenstrual dysphoric disorder if left untreated, particularly when individuals experience environmental stressors. The study validates the Turkish version of the Premenstrual Syndrome-Impact Questionnaire (PMS-IQ/T) as a reliable assessment tool, demonstrating strong correlations with established PMS measurement scales (PMSS and PMSIS) and its association with neuroticism-related symptoms. These findings underscore the importance of standardized, cross-culturally adapted assessment instruments for identifying and evaluating PMS severity across diverse populations. Interventions such as relaxation techniques represent potential management approaches for alleviating PMS symptoms.

title: The impact of telework on absenteeism, presenteeism, and return to work among workers with health conditions: a scoping review
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review examines how telework affects work attendance and return-to-work outcomes among workers with health conditions. Telework consistently reduces sickness absenteeism and facilitates return to work, particularly following surgery or during chronic illness management, by providing workers greater flexibility to manage their health conditions. However, telework's effects on presenteeism (working while sick) are contradictory: while some studies show telework reduces presenteeism in certain conditions like period pain and influenza-like illness, others demonstrate that telework increases presenteeism prevalence, likely due to blurred boundaries between work and personal life that encourage working through illness. Mental health disorders including depression, anxiety, and stress are strongly associated with prolonged sick leave, and telework access may help mitigate this burden. The review highlights that while telework offers substantial benefits for reducing work absences and supporting return-to-work trajectories, its potential to increase presenteeism and contribute to social isolation requires careful implementation and workplace oversight to prevent adverse health outcomes.

title: A long shot? Affective temperaments predict adherence to pharmacotherapy during infertility treatment in a prospective longitudinal study
summary: This prospective longitudinal study demonstrates that affective temperaments significantly predict medication adherence and treatment outcomes in women undergoing infertility treatment. Specifically, higher scores on cyclothymic, depressive, irritable, and anxious affective temperaments were all independently associated with reduced medication adherence during pharmacotherapy, with advancing age intensifying the negative impact of anxious temperament on adherence. The study reveals that affective temperaments influence adherence through their effects on energy levels, mood, emotional reactivity, cognitive patterns, and motivation to comply with medical recommendations, ultimately impacting pregnancy rates and infertility treatment success. Notably, infertility itself is associated with considerable psychological distress and may contribute to depression and anxiety, creating a bidirectional relationship between mental health and treatment outcomes. Additionally, lifestyle factors including balanced diet and regular physical activity enhance fertility, while smoking and excessive alcohol consumption reduce it, suggesting comprehensive approaches to infertility management should address both psychological temperament and behavioral factors alongside pharmacological interventions.

title: Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning in Sexual Health and Dysfunction Across the Cancer Care Continuum: A Systematic Review
summary:
# Summary

Sexual dysfunction is a prevalent and under-addressed complication affecting cancer patients across multiple cancer types, with incidence rates reaching 40–80% in gynecological cancers, up to 80% in prostate cancer, and 55–73% in hematologic cancers. Cancer treatments including surgery, chemotherapy, radiation therapy, and hormonal therapy directly contribute to sexual dysfunction, while psychological factors such as anxiety and depression further exacerbate these outcomes, significantly reducing patient quality of life. Artificial intelligence and machine learning technologies are increasingly being applied to predict and manage sexual dysfunction in cancer care, with common approaches including regression models, gradient boosting models, neural networks, and natural language processing algorithms achieving high performance metrics, exemplified by Random Forest models reaching median AUC values of 0.98. Despite their promise, studies demonstrate substantial methodological limitations, including low adherence to TRIPOD + AI reporting guidelines (60%), universal high risk of bias, and inadequate calibration assessments in 21.4% of studies, highlighting the need for improved research rigor in this emerging field.

title: 24-Hour Movement Behaviour and Health Awareness as Possible Predictors of Infertility-Related Quality of Life
summary:
# Summary

Infertility imposes substantial psychosocial burdens including elevated stress, social stigma, and negative mental health impacts, with 27.8% of women and 41% of women reporting depressive and elevated stress symptoms respectively. Twenty-four-hour movement behaviors significantly predict infertility-related quality of life, with better sleep quality, lower sedentary time, and higher health literacy emerging as positive predictors, while recreational physical activity positively associates with fertility awareness, psychological well-being, and social relationships. Conversely, sedentary time negatively correlates with both psychological well-being and social relationships, and sleep quality problems strongly predict diminished fertility-related quality of life scores. Health literacy serves as a critical determinant of reproductive health outcomes, promoting fertility awareness, recognition of fertile windows, and positive health behaviors, though paradoxically higher fertility awareness shows adverse effects in some contexts. Psychological interventions such as cognitive coping and relaxation therapy improve psychological well-being, reduce anxiety, and lower treatment dropout rates, while social support acts as a protective factor mitigating infertility-related stress and improving treatment tolerance. Multiple biological and lifestyle factors including age, body mass index, treatment duration, and rural residence influence assisted reproductive technology outcomes and overall quality of life.

title: Improving the Quality of Life of Gynecological Patients with Surgery: A Cross-Sectional Study Based on Questionnaire Surveys
summary: This cross-sectional study examined how surgical treatment of gynecological diseases affects patient quality of life across diverse demographic groups. Minimally invasive surgical procedures significantly reduced disease symptoms and improved outcomes, with 92% of women reporting improved daily functioning and 90% experiencing better overall quality of life postoperatively. However, several factors moderated surgical benefits: patients with uterine fibroids showed consistently less improvement across multiple quality-of-life domains including social activities, mobility, and goal achievement compared to those with other conditions like urinary incontinence. Higher body mass index was associated with greater limitations both before and after surgery, while psychological factors including anxiety and long-term distress significantly impacted quality of life outcomes. Notably, the timing and nature of symptom improvement varied by age and life circumstance: younger women (under 35) showed greater improvement in fertility-related outcomes, middle-aged women (36-50) improved most in professional functioning, and older women (over 51) demonstrated greater gains in social participation and sports activities. These findings suggest that while surgical intervention effectively improves gynecological quality of life, individualized approaches considering comorbidities, psychological status, and demographic factors optimize patient outcomes.

title: Consultations for Obstetric and Gynecological Problems in the Pediatric Population in a Tertiary Care Hospital Setting
journal: Journal of Clinical Practice and Research
summary: This tertiary care study examined obstetric and gynecological consultations in a pediatric population, with menstrual disorders representing the predominant clinical issue across adolescent age groups. Menstrual dysfunction manifested as irregular menstruation, dysmenorrhea, and amenorrhea, with dysfunctional uterine bleeding identified as the primary cause, while secondary causes included polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), hypothyroidism, hyperprolactinemia, psychological stress, and eating disorders. Endometriosis emerged as the most common cause of secondary dysmenorrhea in adolescents, though its exact prevalence in this population remains undefined. Beyond menstrual pathology, the study documented significant associations between irregular menstruation and hyperandrogenic features including hirsutism and acne in over half of adolescent patients, while vulvovaginitis and simple ovarian cysts were frequently identified on imaging evaluation. In late adolescence, pregnancy-related consultations became nearly as common as menstrual disorders, with abdominal pain representing the predominant presenting symptom in pregnant adolescents. These findings underscore the clinical complexity of gynecological conditions in the pediatric population and highlight the need for comprehensive evaluation of menstrual disorders and associated endocrine abnormalities in adolescent patients.

title: How Do Korean Women Manage Menstrual or Menopausal Symptoms: 2022 Women’s Health Survey for Sexual and Reproductive Health
summary: This 2022 Korean survey examined menstrual and menopausal symptom prevalence across women's lifespan stages, revealing that dysmenorrhea is significantly associated with underlying uterine pathologies including endometriosis and uterine fibroids. Adolescents commonly reported menstrual pain (76.5%), premenstrual syndrome, and abnormal uterine bleeding, while adult women experienced even higher prevalence rates of these symptoms (77.1% for menstrual pain, 82.9% for premenstrual syndrome), with substantial proportions reporting severe symptoms. Perimenopausal and postmenopausal women predominantly experienced physical or mental fatigue, joint or muscle discomfort, sleep problems, and vaginal dryness, with menopause-associated hormonal changes linked to increased risks for osteoporosis and cardiovascular disease. Management strategies varied across age groups, with painkillers and hospital consultations being primary responses among adults with severe dysmenorrhea, while exercise and health functional foods were frequently used for menopausal symptom relief. The findings underscore that improved access to menstrual health services and menopause management substantially enhances quality of life and sexual and reproductive health outcomes in Korean women.

title: Mental Health Across the Conception Journey: Trying To Conceive Without Treatment, Considering Treatment, and with Treatment
summary:
# Summary

This study examined mental health outcomes across different stages of the conception journey using data from 3,458 individuals using a digital health platform. Contrary to expectations, individuals trying to conceive without treatment reported comparable rates of anxiety, depression, and conception-specific anxiety to those considering or undergoing fertility treatment. Specific conception-related stressors including ovulation tracking, timed intercourse, sex on demand, and menstrual disappointment were all strongly associated with anxiety symptoms. Notably, those seeking fertility treatment were more likely to have endometriosis, polycystic ovary syndrome, thyroid disease, and prior miscarriage history, and expressed significantly greater interest in receiving emotional support compared to those attempting conception naturally. The findings suggest that mental health screening and support should be integrated across primary care and digital health platforms, particularly during routine gynecological appointments, to provide early identification and intervention for the substantial psychological burden experienced throughout the conception journey.

title: Periods and well-being: A focus group study to discuss how menstruation affects the well-being of women aged 18–40
summary: This focus group study examined how menstruation affects the well-being of women aged 18–40, revealing substantial impacts across physical, psychological, and social dimensions. Dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and premenstrual syndrome (PMS) were strongly associated with reduced quality of life and impaired school and work attendance, with endometriosis and adenomyosis identified as key pathological causes of menstrual pain. PMS and its severe form, premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), cause multiple affective symptoms including mood swings, depression, anxiety, and irritability, while also producing somatic effects such as bloating, fatigue, and altered appetite that reduce self-esteem and concentration. Menstruation broadly impacts daily functioning by disrupting sleep, exercise performance, work productivity, and eating habits, though women also recognized positive aspects including its value as a fertility and pregnancy biomarker and its role in fostering connection to womanhood and other women. Stigma surrounding menstruation emerged as a pervasive barrier that contributes to delays in seeking treatment and perpetuates normalization of female pain, while comprehensive menstrual education is identified as critical to reducing misinformation and improving health outcomes. The study underscores that addressing menstrual health requires integrated approaches encompassing clinical management of underlying conditions, psychosocial support, and public education to combat stigma and enhance women's overall well-being.

title: Determining the relationship between sleep quality and reproductive health issues in fertile and infertile men and women: A cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates a significant bidirectional relationship between sleep quality and reproductive health across both sexes. Poor sleep quality was substantially more prevalent in infertile individuals, with 65.9% of infertile women and 36.8% of infertile men reporting poor sleep compared to their fertile counterparts, and sleep disturbance was significantly associated with increased odds of infertility particularly in women. The study identified multiple risk factors for poor sleep quality in fertile women, including older age, menstrual disorders, sexual dysfunction, psychological distress, and history of abortion-inducing drug use, while marital duration of 1–5 years emerged as a protective factor. Notably, infertile women demonstrated substantially higher prevalence of gynecological conditions including endometriosis (20.7%), menstrual disorders (31.9%), and dysmenorrhea (71.9%), alongside elevated rates of psychiatric disorders (14.9% versus 5.1% in fertile women). Women with diminished ovarian reserve were 30 times more likely to have sleep disorders, and over 35% of women undergoing in vitro fertilization reported sleep disturbances, suggesting sleep quality may represent a modifiable target for improving infertility treatment outcomes. Sleep dysfunction is hypothesized to impair reproductive processes through disruption of hormonal regulation, with broader associations documented between sleep disorders and cardiovascular disease, hypertension, depression, anxiety, and obesity.

title: Relationship between premenstrual syndrome symptoms, anthropometric measurements, and eating behaviors in women of childbearing age: Turkish sample
summary:
# Summary

Premenstrual syndrome triggers diverse physical and psychological symptoms—including appetite changes, weight gain, pain, anxiety, irritability, and fatigue—that substantially alter eating behaviors in women of childbearing age. Hormonal fluctuations during the menstrual cycle disrupt appetite control, leading to increased uncontrolled eating, emotional eating, and cognitive restriction, particularly in women with higher premenstrual symptom severity. Among the specific symptom correlations, appetite changes showed the strongest association with uncontrolled eating, while fatigue, anxiety, and depressive symptoms also significantly correlated with maladaptive eating patterns. Body mass index, age, and premenstrual syndrome severity jointly predicted eating behavior disturbances, with obesity identified as a notable risk factor for PMS development. Dietary interventions including complex carbohydrates with high fiber content and adequate vitamin D intake demonstrated potential in improving premenstrual symptom severity. These findings underscore the bidirectional relationship between hormonal cyclicity, symptom manifestation, and eating behavior dysregulation in reproductive-aged women.

title: The association between various menstrual disorders and well-being was mediated by anxiety: a cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study reveals that anxiety functions as a critical mediator linking menstrual disorders to reduced well-being. While premenstrual symptoms show both direct and indirect effects on well-being, menstrual pain, intermenstrual bleeding, and abnormal light bleeding affect well-being exclusively through anxiety pathways. The underlying mechanism involves hormonal fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone that dysregulate neurotransmitters like serotonin and gamma-aminobutyric acid, with stress and anxiety further exacerbating menstrual irregularities through hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis activation. Supporting lifestyle factors—including adequate sleep and financial security—significantly improve well-being, while depressive symptoms compound its deterioration. These findings suggest that anxiety-focused psychological interventions targeting individuals with menstrual disorders may effectively improve overall well-being and quality of life.

title: The efficacy of psychological interventions for infertile women: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: Infertility profoundly impacts women's mental health, causing elevated rates of anxiety (60% higher risk) and depression (40% higher risk) compared to fertile women, alongside associated emotional symptoms including grief, shame, anger, and loneliness, as well as somatic manifestations such as fatigue, tension, and sleep disruption. The psychological burden extends to identity confusion and chronic stress, potentially mediated by neurobiological stress pathways activated by prolonged uncertainty and perceived loss of control. This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that psychological interventions significantly reduce anxiety and depression symptoms in infertile women while improving overall well-being and pregnancy outcomes, with cognitive-behavioral therapy and counseling showing particular efficacy. Treatment effectiveness is optimized by increasing the number of sessions rather than extending individual session duration or overall intervention length, with online delivery formats and regional factors also moderating treatment effects. Emerging psychological approaches including Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing and Acceptance and Commitment Therapy expand the therapeutic toolkit available to address infertility-related distress. The findings support integrating targeted psychological care directly into fertility treatment protocols to comprehensively address both the biological and psychological dimensions of infertility.

title: Quality of life of women with gestational diabetes mellitus in a tertiary care hospital, Ajman, UAE: A cross-sectional descriptive study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study assessed quality of life (QOL) in 180 women with gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) in the UAE, finding that 77.8% experienced moderate QOL while 22.2% reported high QOL. GDM significantly compromises QOL through physical, psychological, and social challenges, with anxiety about high-risk pregnancy complications—including hypertension, macrosomia, and cesarean delivery—emerging as the dominant concern (mean score 29.59). Women with previous GDM history or prior obstetric complications demonstrated substantially lower QOL scores, while those with higher educational attainment reported improved outcomes. The study identified critical barriers to well-being, including restrictive dietary requirements, limited physical activity, and cultural factors that complicate disease management, though adequate healthcare access and support systems partially mitigated severe QOL deterioration. Integrating culturally tailored health education, psychological support, and enhanced social support alongside pharmacological treatment (insulin and metformin) emerged as essential interventions to reduce fear-driven stress and improve coping and self-management in this vulnerable population.

title: Menstrual-related symptoms and absence from school among young people in Sweden: a stratified, randomized, population-based survey
summary:
# Summary

This population-based Swedish survey examined how menstrual-related symptoms impact school attendance among young people, finding that dysmenorrhea, heavy bleeding, mood changes, and other complaints such as headache and concentration difficulties all significantly associate with school absence, with the latter showing the strongest correlation. Beyond school absenteeism, menstrual symptoms substantially impair quality of life, participation in work and school, intimate relationships, and physical activity, while also perpetuating gender inequalities in health and well-being. Early menarche (before age 11), long-term health conditions, foreign-born status, and lower parental education were identified as risk factors for increased symptom severity and school absence. The study emphasizes that menstrual stigma, social silence, and normalization of symptoms create barriers to care-seeking, allowing treatable conditions like dysmenorrhea and menorrhagia to persist untreated and unnecessarily disrupt adolescent development and educational progression.

title: The medicalisation of menstruation: a double-edged sword
summary:
# Summary

This article examines the historical and contemporary medicalization of menstruation and its paradoxical effects on women's mental health and wellbeing. From the 19th century onwards, psychiatry established strong associations between menstrual events—including menarche, disruption, and cessation—and mental disorders, creating distinct diagnostic categories like "ovarian insanity" and "amenorrhoeal insanity" that pathologized normal reproductive biology. The medicalization expanded in the 20th century with widespread hormone replacement therapy (HRT) adoption to treat menopausal symptoms through estrogen supplementation, though subsequent research linking HRT to breast cancer and stroke dramatically reduced its prescription. The article highlights that contemporary conditions like premenstrual syndrome (PMS) demonstrate how social and relational factors—particularly negative partner constructions of symptoms—substantially amplify distress, and that couple-based cognitive behavioral therapy proves more effective than individual treatment. Modern interventions including menstrual leave and menstrual product policies attempt to reduce menstrual burden, yet the article ultimately critiques how the medicalization framework, while addressing legitimate symptoms, perpetuates problematic associations between women's reproductive functions and mental instability that originated in outdated biological theories.

title: Physical and psychological needs of women undergoing hysterectomy: A literature review
summary: Hysterectomy is a widely performed surgical treatment for multiple gynecological conditions including fibroids, endometriosis, uterine cancer, prolapse, and abnormal bleeding, but the procedure carries significant physical and psychological consequences for patients. Beyond immediate postoperative complications such as pain and infection risk, hysterectomy and particularly concurrent ovarian removal trigger substantial hormonal changes that produce symptoms including hot flashes, vaginal dryness, and fatigue, which may be managed through hormone replacement therapy. Women frequently experience considerable psychological distress following hysterectomy, including sadness, loneliness, identity uncertainty, body image concerns, and grief related to loss of reproductive capacity and diminished self-worth. Pre-operative education, comprehensive counseling, emotional support, peer support groups, and post-operative discussion significantly improve mental well-being and reduce perioperative anxiety and confusion in affected women. Physical limitations following surgery further compound emotional challenges, underscoring the importance of integrated pain management and physical therapy in supporting both physical recovery and sexual health restoration. Taken together, successful hysterectomy outcomes require holistic multidisciplinary support addressing the interconnected physical, hormonal, and psychological needs of women throughout their surgical journey.

title: Menstrual health among young adults in Latin America and the Caribbean: A scoping review and evidence-gap map
summary:
# Summary

This scoping review examines menstrual health among young adults in Latin America and the Caribbean, revealing substantial impacts on physical, mental, and social well-being. Dysmenorrhea and premenstrual syndrome are highly prevalent conditions, with dysmenorrhea affecting 52-79% of Mexican university students and premenstrual syndrome occurring in up to 50% of Brazilian students, both causing significant interference with daily functioning. These menstrual conditions directly impair academic performance through concentration difficulties and absenteeism, reduce social participation and exercise engagement, and negatively affect intimate and family relationships, with 59% of women with premenstrual syndrome reporting impacts on partner relationships. Mental health symptoms are prominent, including anxiety, irritability, and depression associated with the menstrual cycle and premenstrual syndrome. Treatment approaches vary regionally, from pharmaceutical interventions like paracetamol and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs in urban areas to alternative and complementary treatments in rural Peru, with relaxation techniques supplementing medication in Brazil. The review underscores that menstrual health is a critical determinant of overall well-being and identifies significant gaps in healthcare access and support systems across the region.

title: The impact of 4-week high-intensity interval training on mental health and sleep quality in female college students with normal weight obesity: a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

A 4-week high-intensity interval training (HIIT) intervention significantly improved mental health and sleep quality in female college students with normal weight obesity, a metabolic condition characterized by elevated cardiometabolic risk despite normal body mass index. The HIIT program substantially reduced depressive symptoms (PHQ-9 scores decreased from 5.8 to 3.2, p<0.01) and anxiety symptoms (GAD-7 scores decreased from 5.1 to 2.8, p<0.05), while also enhancing sleep quality measured by the Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index (PSQI scores improved from 5.1 to 3.5). Strong positive correlations emerged between improved sleep quality and reduced depression and anxiety symptoms post-intervention, suggesting that sleep improvement may mediate the mental health benefits of exercise. The control group showed no significant changes in any mental health or sleep measures, confirming the intervention's efficacy. These findings demonstrate that HIIT effectively breaks the pathological cycle linking normal weight obesity, sedentary behavior, psychological distress, and poor sleep quality by simultaneously addressing cardiovascular fitness, body composition, and mental health outcomes.

title: Development and validation of a premenstrual symptom screening tool for working women in relation to absenteeism
summary:
# Summary

This study developed and validated a premenstrual syndrome (PMS) screening tool specifically for working women to identify those at risk for work absenteeism. The screening scale effectively characterizes PMS across four distinct symptom dimensions: somatic symptoms (dizziness, palpitations, joint pain, chest pain, tinnitus, and numbness), psychological symptoms (mood depression, anxiety, irritability, and concentration problems), reduced work efficiency (increased errors, prolonged task completion, lower accomplishment), and abdominal symptoms (pain, cramps, bloating, and gastrointestinal disturbances). The validated scale demonstrates moderate predictive ability for work absenteeism and correlates with burnout measures, supporting its utility for clinical screening. PMS and its severe form, premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), significantly impair quality of life and contribute to substantial workplace productivity losses through both absenteeism and presenteeism, with workplace stressors further exacerbating symptom severity. This screening tool addresses a critical occupational health need, as premenstrual symptoms—particularly psychological and autonomic manifestations—elevate subjective stress and negatively impact job performance in a substantial proportion of working women aged 18–41 years.

title: Hypothalamic amenorrhoea as an extraintestinal manifestation of Crohn's disease: A case report
journal: Malaysian Family Physician : the Official Journal of the Academy of Family Physicians of Malaysia
summary: Crohn's disease causes secondary amenorrhoea and reduced fertility through suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis via three interconnected mechanisms: chronic intestinal inflammation, malnutrition-induced leptin deficiency, and psychological stress. Elevated inflammatory cytokines including IL-1, IL-6, and TNF-α directly impair gonadotropin secretion, while malnutrition and low body mass index suppress leptin production, which is essential for maintaining gonadotropin-releasing hormone pulsatility and normal ovulation. Menstrual dysfunction occurs in up to 30% of women with inflammatory bowel disease during active disease phases, though this condition is reversible with appropriate disease management and nutritional rehabilitation. Combined oral contraceptive pills carry particular risks in Crohn's disease patients by exacerbating systemic inflammation and increasing disease relapse by 46% through estrogen-mediated shifts toward T-helper 2-mediated immune responses. This case illustrates that functional hypothalamic amenorrhoea represents an important extraintestinal manifestation of Crohn's disease that should prompt investigation of underlying nutritional status and disease control, with restoration of menses achievable through corticosteroid therapy and nutritional rehabilitation.

title: Gender-specific association between health-related quality of life and sexual function in colorectal cancer patients in Iran: a descriptive-correlational study
summary: Colorectal cancer significantly impairs both health-related quality of life (HRQoL) and sexual function through direct disease effects and cancer treatments including surgery, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, and particularly ostomy placement. In female patients, physical functioning, energy levels, and social functioning showed strong positive correlations with multiple aspects of sexual health, including sexual satisfaction, desire, orgasm, and lubrication, while also correlating with reduced sexual pain. General HRQoL demonstrated positive associations with sexual satisfaction in women, and higher social functioning was linked to improved sexual health outcomes in both genders. The study reveals important gender-specific patterns, with energy levels showing opposing associations in males versus females, suggesting that HRQoL domains influence sexual function through distinct pathways by gender. These findings underscore the bidirectional relationship between HRQoL and sexual function in colorectal cancer survivors, highlighting the importance of comprehensive quality-of-life interventions that address physical capabilities, psychological well-being, and social support to improve sexual health outcomes.

title: E-Health Intervention for Fear of Cancer Recurrence
summary:
# Summary

Fear of cancer recurrence (FCR) is a highly prevalent and debilitating psychological condition affecting cancer survivors that is strongly associated with anxiety, depression, health anxiety, and increased healthcare utilization. The therapist-guided e-health intervention TG-iConquerFear significantly reduces FCR severity, anxiety, depression, and emotional distress while simultaneously improving quality of life and reducing the burden of physical symptoms in cancer survivors. The intervention works through multiple mechanisms, including reducing negative beliefs about worry, lowering perceived risk of recurrence, and diminishing psychological triggers and functional impairments. Key process improvements include enhanced awareness of bodily symptoms and gains in insight into the nature of fear responses. Therapist guidance emerged as a critical factor in enhancing both adherence and engagement with the intervention, with 86% completion rates in the guided format compared to 36% in self-directed formats, demonstrating that digital mental health interventions for cancer-related psychological distress achieve stronger outcomes with professional support.

title: Dyadic Mental Health in Paediatric Congenital Heart Disease: Actor–Partner Associations Between Child HRQoL/Depression and Caregiver Stress Across Lesion Severity
summary:
# Summary

This study examines bidirectional psychological relationships between children with congenital heart disease and their caregivers, revealing a robust "dyadic" effect where each member's mental health influences the other's. Cardiac lesion severity directly reduces both child health-related quality of life (PedsQL scores decreased from 81.2 in mild cases to 63.3 in severe cases) and caregiver mental health (SF-36 Mental Component Summary declined from 66.8 to 54.1), with child depressive symptoms and caregiver stress mediating these effects. Notably, caregiver stress independently predicts reduced child quality of life beyond anatomical severity, while child depression correspondingly diminishes caregiver mental health—demonstrating bidirectional actor-partner associations. Unplanned hospitalizations amplify this psychological synchrony, substantially worsening both child and caregiver outcomes across both sexes and parental roles. Targeted interventions such as dyadic cognitive-behavioral therapy, mindfulness-based stress reduction, and structured family exercise programs show promise in interrupting these negative feedback loops and improving outcomes for both dyad members.

title: Perspectives and experiences of physical activity among autistic adults in middle adulthood
summary:
# Summary

Autistic adults in middle adulthood face significant health inequalities and reduced physical activity participation compared to neurotypical peers, yet physical activity offers substantial protective benefits including improved cardiovascular fitness, sleep quality, mental well-being, and reduced anxiety and stress. The primary barriers to physical activity engagement stem from neuro-normative assumptions and bio-medical conceptualizations of autism that emphasize bodily aesthetics and performance maximization rather than individualized wellness, combined with movement professionals' limited understanding of autistic needs and sensory sensitivities. Key facilitators for physical activity participation include trauma-informed approaches that build trust, accommodation of sensory factors, and supportive environments that recognize individual variation rather than imposing standardized exercise paradigms.
