The misalignment between dominant societal logics of physical activity—focused on appearance and competitive performance—and autistic adults' actual motivations for movement and mental health creates apathy toward traditional exercise formats. Addressing these barriers requires shifting from neuro-normative frameworks to individualized, neurodivergent-affirming approaches to physical activity that prioritize enjoyment, social inclusion, and mental health benefits specific to autistic populations.

title: The relationship between stress, anxiety and depression with sexual function among women with premature ovarian insufficiency in the West of Iran
summary:
# Summary

This study examined how psychological factors affect sexual function in women with premature ovarian insufficiency (POI), a condition characterized by ovarian failure before age 40. POI results from hormonal dysregulation (elevated FSH, reduced AMH) that causes irregular menstruation, vasomotor symptoms, dyspareunia, and infertility, while also triggering profound psychological consequences including depression, anxiety, and diminished self-esteem. The findings revealed that depression, anxiety, and stress are strongly intercorrelated (0.48–0.57) and robustly associated with reduced sexual function across multiple domains, including decreased desire, arousal difficulties, and sexual dissatisfaction. Longer infertility duration exacerbated both psychological symptoms and sexual dysfunction, and lower economic status and family history of infertility were linked to worse sexual outcomes. The study demonstrates a cascade effect wherein POI-related challenges—sense of maternal failure, body shame, and negative body image—progressively lead to anxiety and depression, which in turn significantly impair sexual relationships. Psychological counseling is recommended as an intervention to address these interconnected mental health and sexual function issues in this population.

title: Effectiveness of pelvic myofascial trigger point release for the therapy of sexual dysfunction in women after vaginal delivery: A prospective pilot study
journal: Turkish Journal of Physical Medicine and Rehabilitation
summary: Vaginal delivery causes postpartum female sexual dysfunction through mechanical trauma and hormonal changes affecting pelvic floor tissue, with pain during intercourse, reduced desire, vaginal dryness, and orgasmic dysfunction being common sequelae. Postpartum pelvic floor muscles become susceptible to fatigue and poor coordination, and heightened muscle tension contributes to urogenital pain and sexual dysfunction. This pilot study demonstrated that pelvic myofascial trigger point release combined with structured pelvic floor muscle training (PFMT) effectively treats postpartum sexual dysfunction by reducing muscle fatigue, decreasing pelvic floor muscle tension, and alleviating pain. Treatment significantly improved all six dimensions of the Female Sexual Function Index—sexual desire, arousal, lubrication, orgasmic function, satisfaction, and pain reduction—compared to control groups receiving standard PFMT alone. Electromyography measurements confirmed substantial reductions in pre-resting muscle tension values in the treatment group, supporting the mechanistic role of myofascial release in restoring normal pelvic floor physiology. These findings suggest that combining myofascial trigger point release with structured PFMT offers superior outcomes for addressing postpartum sexual dysfunction compared to exercise alone.

title: Causal relationship between subhealth status and pan-cancer risk: A Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study demonstrates that multiple dimensions of subhealth status—characterized by mental health disturbances, reduced physiological resilience, and poor health satisfaction—are causally linked to elevated cancer risk across multiple tumor types. Psychological factors including irritability, mood swings, and low health satisfaction show significant associations with lung cancer, basal-cell carcinoma, cervical cancer, and pancreatic cancer, respectively, with heart rate variability serving as a biomarker of autonomic nervous system dysfunction that predicts colorectal cancer risk. The study elucidates a mechanistic pathway whereby these psychological and physiological disturbances promote chronic inflammation through reduced vagal nerve activity and parasympathetic nervous system tone, ultimately facilitating tumorigenesis via DNA damage, oxidative stress, and tumor microenvironment remodeling. Additional pathways include irritability-driven gastroesophageal reflux disease leading to systemic inflammation, obesity-associated adipokine dysregulation, and irritable bowel syndrome-related dysbiosis. The study identifies several preventive and therapeutic interventions—including meditation training and slow breathing exercises that enhance vagal activity and heart rate variability, along with cognitive behavioral therapy and mindfulness meditation for psychological symptom management—as potential strategies to mitigate cancer risk by restoring autonomic balance and reducing chronic inflammation. These findings underscore the importance of addressing subhealth states and psychological well-being as integral components of cancer prevention strategies.

title: Exploring the dimensions of discrimination in emergency department services: a qualitative study
journal: International Journal for Equity in Health
summary: This qualitative study examines how discrimination in emergency department services creates a cascade of physiological and psychological harms with particular relevance to vulnerable populations experiencing conditions like endometriosis. Discrimination directly triggers chronic stress, which elevates glucocorticoid levels and subsequently impairs multiple body systems including immune function, glycemic control, and infection resistance. Beyond these physiological pathways, discrimination also impairs cognitive function and contributes to psychological disorders, while specifically undermining adequate pain assessment and management—a critical concern for endometriosis patients who already face delayed diagnoses and inadequate treatment. The study demonstrates that discriminatory practices erode patient trust in healthcare systems, leading to reduced healthcare utilization and cumulative increases in overall health risks. These findings highlight how structural inequities in emergency care compound the clinical burden experienced by endometriosis patients, who often face intersecting discrimination based on gender, race, or other marginalized identities. Addressing discrimination in emergency departments is therefore essential not only for equitable care but also for preventing stress-related physiological complications that exacerbate endometriosis-related morbidity.

title: Technology-Related Trauma in Sexual and Reproductive Health Digital Technologies: Grounded Theory Study
summary:
# Summary

This grounded theory study identifies a critical clinical concern: digital health technologies designed for sexual and reproductive health services can inadvertently cause technology-related trauma through multiple pathways. Platform design features, user interface elements, and interactive components trigger emotional trauma in users, with particular risk for individuals with prior sexual or reproductive trauma who experience retraumatization through exposure to explicit imagery, videos, or vulgar language. The study reveals that stigmatized health services like STI testing, contraception, and abortion care become additionally harmful when delivered through technology, as negative digital experiences perpetuate prior trauma. Specific vulnerabilities include surveillance mechanisms in menstrual tracking apps, potential exploitation by intimate partners through spyware installation, and negative interpersonal interactions mediated by digital platforms. The findings underscore that seemingly neutral design elements and technology-based interventions for HIV adherence and other reproductive health needs can generate psychological distress even in users without prior trauma, suggesting that comprehensive redesign of these platforms with trauma-informed principles is essential for ensuring patient safety in digital sexual and reproductive health services.

title: Effects of dysmenorrhea on work productivity and quality of life in Japanese women: A large-scale web-based cross-sectional study
summary: This large-scale web-based study demonstrates that dysmenorrhea severity significantly impairs work productivity and quality of life in Japanese women across multiple domains. Dysmenorrhea severity was independently associated with increased absenteeism, presenteeism, and overall work impairment, as well as reductions in physical, mental, and social quality of life components, even after adjusting for confounding factors. Socioeconomic factors also played important roles, with low household income linked to diminished work productivity and mental/social well-being, while urban residence was associated with greater work impairment compared to rural areas. Comorbid conditions including asthma, heart disease, and allergies were more prevalent in women with severe dysmenorrhea and contributed to further reductions in quality of life and work capacity. The study underscores dysmenorrhea as a significant public health concern affecting prime working-age women, with implications extending beyond menstrual pain to encompass broad occupational and psychosocial functioning. These findings support the clinical importance of managing dysmenorrhea severity, particularly in populations with low income and urban residence, to improve overall quality of life and work productivity.

title: Effect of health education on mood and pregnancy rate among infertile patients undergoing assisted reproduction: A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This meta-analysis demonstrates that structured health education interventions significantly improve psychological and clinical outcomes in infertile patients undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART). Infertility is inherently associated with substantial psychological distress, including anxiety and depression, which are exacerbated by treatment uncertainty and societal pressure and subsequently impair ART success rates and treatment adherence. Health education interventions effectively reduce these psychological burdens by enhancing cognitive understanding, health awareness, treatment confidence, and patient satisfaction across all stages of ART. The analysis of 1,324 participants across 11 studies revealed that patients receiving health education demonstrated significantly higher health cognition scores, health knowledge awareness, and patient satisfaction compared to control groups. Additionally, psychological interventions, including counseling, were associated with improved pregnancy outcomes and reduced anxiety and depression symptoms. These findings underscore the critical role of integrating health education and psychological support into ART protocols to optimize both mental health and reproductive success in infertile patients.

title: The ART of Embryo Selection: A Review of Methods to Rank the Most Competent Embryo(s) for Transfer to Optimize IVF Success
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive review examines embryo selection methods in assisted reproductive technology to optimize IVF success rates. Multiple factors compromise embryo competence, including maternal age-associated aneuploidy, oocyte cytoplasmic abnormalities, and declined endometrial gene expression linked to ciliogenesis downregulation in women over 35. Paternal factors similarly impact outcomes, with advanced paternal age and abnormal sperm morphology reducing embryo formation and euploidy rates through disrupted epigenetic regulation via histone modifications and DNA methylation. Systemic conditions including endometriosis, PCOS with associated hormone imbalance, elevated BMI, and lifestyle factors (psychological stress, smoking, alcohol) significantly hinder ovarian response and fertility. Morphological assessment of developing embryos—evaluating blastomere symmetry, fragmentation, blastocoel expansion, trophectoderm cell quality, and inner cell mass characteristics—predicts implantation success, with day-5 blastocysts demonstrating superior implantation rates compared to cleavage-stage transfers. Preimplantation genetic testing for aneuploidy (PGT-A), enhanced by next-generation sequencing, reduces miscarriage risk and improves pregnancy success, though mosaicism and technical limitations like allele dropout can affect accuracy.

title: The Relationship Between Premenstrual Dysphoric Symptoms, Perceived Stress, and Sleep Quality Among Nursing Students: A Cross-Sectional Study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study examined the interconnected relationships between premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD), perceived stress, and sleep quality in nursing students. PMDD is fundamentally driven by fluctuations in sex steroid hormones (estrogen and progesterone), with contributing factors including genetics, central nervous system sensitivity, and serotonergic dysfunction; the condition manifests approximately one week before menstruation with symptoms including irritability, anxiety, mood instability, headache, breast tenderness, and bloating. The study identified significant positive correlations between premenstrual symptoms and perceived stress (r=0.39) and between perceived stress and sleep disturbance (r=0.28), establishing a pathophysiological pathway wherein menstrual cycle-related hormonal changes disrupt sleep patterns through elevated body temperature and altered neurotransmitter function, ultimately amplifying perceived stress. Younger female students exhibited higher premenstrual symptom severity with greater symptom awareness, while sleep disturbances were broadly associated with menstrual complications including dysmenorrhea and abnormal cycles. The findings underscore that PMDD creates substantial functional impairment affecting academic performance, social relationships, and quality of life, highlighting the clinical importance of addressing the stress-sleep-premenstrual symptom triad in affected populations.

title: Factors affecting health-related quality of life in women with and without abnormal uterine bleeding: an unmatched case-control study
summary:
# Summary

Abnormal uterine bleeding substantially reduces health-related quality of life and sexual quality of life in affected women while simultaneously increasing sexual distress, a relationship mediated through inverse correlations between sexual distress and overall wellbeing. The severity of menstrual abnormalities compounds this effect, with women experiencing both irregular periods and heavy bleeding or menstrual cycles exceeding ten days showing the lowest quality of life scores. Multiple physiological mechanisms underlie these outcomes, including iron deficiency anemia, chronic fatigue, pelvic pain, and hormonal imbalances stemming from hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis instability and altered progesterone-estrogen ratios. Beyond physical consequences, abnormal bleeding triggers psychological distress manifested as anxiety, diminished libido, negative body image changes, and fear of cancer, ultimately perpetuating cycles of emotional isolation and relational discord. Sociodemographic factors including higher education and younger age correlate with better outcomes, suggesting that comprehensive management—combining treatment of underlying causes such as endometriosis and fibroids with medical interventions, counseling, and patient education—can meaningfully improve quality of life in affected women.

title: Menstrual Characteristics Associated With Successful Pregnancy Within 6 Months Among Women Planning to Conceive
summary:
# Summary

This study identified specific menstrual characteristics associated with faster conception among women planning to become pregnant. Women with menstrual cycles of 27–32 days, bleeding lasting 5–6 days, ovulation signs occurring on days 13–16, and absence of dysmenorrhea and blood clots demonstrated significantly higher pregnancy rates and shorter time-to-conception compared to peers with irregular cycles or menstrual abnormalities. Dysmenorrhea and menstrual blood clots emerged as indicators of underlying uterine health problems including endometriosis, adenomyosis, and fibroids, and nearly 50% of participants with these symptoms exhibited substantially reduced fertility rates. The research demonstrates that reproductive hormones regulate cycle stability through the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis, while menstrual pain and clotting reflect compromised uterine circulatory health that impairs implantation. The authors recommend that women use basal body temperature monitoring and luteinizing hormone test strips to identify fertile windows, and suggest lifestyle interventions including weight management, appropriate exercise, and adequate sleep to reduce menstrual symptoms and enhance natural conception rates.

title: The effect of self-care training on death anxiety in hemodialysis patients: A randomized clinical trial
journal: Journal of Education and Health Promotion
summary:
# Summary

This randomized clinical trial demonstrates that self-care training significantly reduces death anxiety in hemodialysis patients, with intervention group scores declining from 8.04 to 5.28 on the Death Anxiety Scale. Death anxiety represents a major psychological burden in hemodialysis patients, particularly affecting females and those over 75 years old, and substantially diminishes quality of life and psychological well-being while increasing mortality risk. Multiple psychosocial interventions—including cognitive behavioral therapy, spiritual care programs, family-centered empowerment models, and educational approaches—effectively reduce death anxiety through mechanisms that enhance illness perception and psychological awareness. Self-care training achieves its therapeutic effects by increasing patient knowledge and awareness of their condition, promoting treatment adherence, and enhancing hope and overall quality of care. The reciprocal relationship between self-care ability and death anxiety reduction suggests that empowering hemodialysis patients with practical self-management skills and comprehensive counseling represents an accessible, efficient clinical strategy for addressing this prevalent psychological complication of chronic kidney disease.

title: INFORM: A Pediatrician's Communication Curriculum About Diagnostic Conversations in Somatic Symptom and Related Disorders
journal: MedEdPORTAL : the Journal of Teaching and Learning Resources
summary:
# Summary

Somatic symptom and related disorders (SSRDs) are characterized by pain, neurologic symptoms, and gastrointestinal symptoms that cause significant distress and impairment in patients. The INFORM curriculum addresses a critical gap in pediatric training: most residents lack formal education in diagnostic communication for SSRDs and incorrectly view them as diagnoses of exclusion, contributing to adverse healthcare experiences and delayed treatment. The curriculum demonstrates that early, confident diagnostic communication with parental buy-in is essential for recovery, as delays in diagnosis and hesitant framing of SSRDs substantially worsen outcomes. After curriculum participation, participants showed a 54% increase in confidence delivering SSRD diagnoses and significant improvements in knowledge about appropriate reassurance strategies, with 79% reporting that role-play practice effectively developed their communication skills. The study highlights that clinician bias and lack of confidence exacerbate patient outcomes and that hospitalizations, testing, and procedures can paradoxically worsen SSRD presentations. This educational intervention demonstrates that targeted communication training equips pediatricians to deliver SSRD diagnoses effectively while maintaining parental engagement and improving treatment trajectories.

title: Five years after the COVID-19 pandemic began: lessons on vaccination and obstetric care in Brazil
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: During the COVID-19 pandemic, Brazil experienced significant increases in maternal and perinatal mortality, caesarean delivery rates, and preterm births, with pregnant women identified as a high-risk group for severe SARS-CoV-2 outcomes, particularly during the postpartum period and among those with comorbidities including obesity, diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. The pandemic's psychological toll was substantial, with over half of pregnant women in São Paulo developing anxiety symptoms associated with post-traumatic stress disorder, while 38.8% experienced postpartum depression and 14.3% reported suicidal ideation, exacerbated by social isolation, disrupted support networks, and intimate partner violence linked to job loss and food insecurity. COVID-19 vaccination emerged as a critical protective intervention, significantly reducing maternal mortality ratio, severe disease, intensive care admissions, maternal sepsis, and neonatal complications, with CoronaVac and mRNA-based vaccines demonstrating high effectiveness. Vaccine acceptance in Brazil was highest among women who perceived personal risk, trusted healthcare providers and official information sources, and believed in vaccine safety and effectiveness, whereas hesitancy was driven by safety concerns, misinformation, and low disease risk perception. Resource limitations disrupted access to obstetric care and delayed diagnosis of complications, while healthcare providers proved instrumental in countering vaccine hesitancy. The pandemic's intersectional impact disproportionately affected vulnerable populations including Black women and those in peri-urban areas with limited access to primary healthcare and referral hospitals.

title: Considerations of Fertility in Elite Sportswomen: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Elite female athletes experience significantly higher rates of menstrual disturbance, including amenorrhea, oligomenorrhea, and anovulatory cycles, primarily driven by relative energy deficiency in sport (REDS) and the associated suppression of the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis. Low energy availability reduces pulsatile gonadotropin-releasing hormone secretion, subsequently lowering luteinizing hormone and follicle-stimulating hormone levels, which leads to estrogen deficiency and impaired ovarian function. While moderate-intensity exercise appears protective for fertility, vigorous high-intensity physical activity—particularly when combined with inadequate nutritional intake—reduces fecundability and increases ovulatory infertility risk, though notably, prior amenorrhea or being underweight during athletic careers does not necessarily predict later conception problems. Restoring energy balance can reestablish menstruation and enhance fertility potential, though some athletes may require assisted reproductive treatment. The article underscores that menstrual disturbance is especially common in sports emphasizing lean body types and that maternal age remains a significant independent factor affecting fertility outcomes, with exponential decline in oocyte quality occurring from the mid-thirties onward.

title: The experiences and informational needs of women electing bariatric surgery: A qualitative content analysis of an online support forum
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative analysis of an online support forum examined the experiences and information needs of women undergoing bariatric surgery, with particular attention to reproductive and metabolic health outcomes. Women reported that bariatric surgery effectively treated obesity and improved associated metabolic conditions like type 2 diabetes, while also reducing psychiatric symptoms including anxiety and depression and enhancing body image. However, the surgery frequently triggered adverse menstrual and reproductive symptoms, including abnormal bleeding, premenstrual syndrome, menstrual pain, and vaginal issues, as well as gastrointestinal complications such as dumping syndrome, nausea, and fatigue. Weight loss following bariatric surgery improved obesity-associated reproductive disorders including polycystic ovary syndrome, anovulation, and menstrual irregularity by correcting hormonal imbalances, thereby potentially restoring fertility. The findings underscore that while bariatric surgery provides significant metabolic and psychological benefits, women experience complex reproductive and physical side effects requiring comprehensive clinical counseling, and online peer support communities serve as valuable resources for women navigating these multifaceted health changes.

title: Oral nutritional supplement compliance among hospitalized cancer patients: a cross-sectional study based on the KAP model
summary: This cross-sectional study examined compliance with oral nutritional supplements (ONS) among hospitalized cancer patients using a knowledge-attitude-practice (KAP) framework. Malnutrition in cancer patients carries substantial clinical consequences, including extended hospital stays averaging 5.20 days, increased healthcare costs of $15,722, 35% higher mortality risk, and 42% increased complications within one year post-chemotherapy, alongside reduced functional status, fatigue, and quality of life. Oral nutritional supplements effectively improve nutritional status and clinical outcomes by preserving skeletal muscle mass, reducing fatigue, and enhancing tolerance to radiotherapy and chemotherapy, with poor compliance negating these benefits and elevating medical costs. The study identified multiple determinants of ONS compliance: social support, self-efficacy, knowledge, and positive attitudes promoted adherence, while tumor type, treatment stage, educational level, and economic status significantly influenced compliance rates. Demographic and psychosocial factors—including age, education, family accompaniment, satisfaction with care, and adverse effects—directly associated with patient knowledge, attitudes, and actual practices regarding ONS use. These findings underscore that improving cancer patient compliance with nutritional supplementation requires multifaceted interventions addressing knowledge gaps, enhancing social support, and managing adverse effects to optimize nutritional outcomes and quality of life.

title: Exploring the role of menstrual perceptions on health, well-being, and daily functioning
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that menstrual perceptions significantly impact physical symptoms and psychological well-being in individuals with menstrual cycle disorders. Positive perceptions of menstruation are associated with lower reported pain levels, reduced disruption to daily activities, and fewer mental health symptoms including depression and anxiety, while feeling empowered by one's menstrual cycle correlates with decreased tearfulness, social withdrawal, difficulty concentrating, and mood disturbances. Menstrual symptoms create substantial life disruption, affecting 90.71% of participants' work performance and 84.31% of intimate relationships, though comfort in seeking support from both healthcare professionals and partners significantly reduces these disruptions and improves satisfaction with management strategies. The menstrual cycle itself influences mood, cognitive function, and conditions like premenstrual syndrome and premenstrual dysphoric disorder, and can be disrupted by modifiable factors including stress, nutrition, exercise, and sleep. Notably, higher self-rated menstrual knowledge is associated with more positive perceptions and empowerment, leading to reduced symptom severity and disruption, suggesting that education and psychological approaches may complement medical interventions in managing menstrual health disorders.

title: Bladder prolapse (cystocele) among African, Asian, and Middle Eastern women: a clinical and socio-cultural perspective
summary:
# Summary

Bladder prolapse (cystocele) represents a significant health burden among African, Asian, and Middle Eastern women, with prevalence rates reaching 20-60% on clinical examination. The condition arises from multiple interconnected risk factors including high parity, early childbirth, prolonged labor, obesity, and gestational diabetes, compounded by sociocultural practices such as early marriage, frequent pregnancies, and delivery without skilled assistance. Cystocele produces substantial urological symptoms—stress urinary incontinence, urinary urgency, incomplete bladder emptying, and recurrent infections—alongside pelvic pressure, vaginal bulging, and dyspareunia that profoundly impact sexual function and mental health, driving anxiety, depression, and social isolation. In resource-limited settings, delayed diagnosis and care-seeking due to cultural stigma and shame often results in advanced disease presentations with vaginal ulceration and chronic complications. Although conservative treatments such as pelvic floor muscle training and pessary fitting effectively manage early-stage prolapse, these interventions remain substantially underutilized due to insufficient staff training and limited access to surgical repair when needed. Strengthening frontline healthcare capacity to detect prolapse early and provide evidence-based conservative management represents a critical priority for addressing this burden.

title: Different faces of anxiety in sexual dysfunction: key features, effective interventions, and critical implications for health care professionals—ESSM position statements
summary:
# Summary

Anxiety represents a pivotal mechanism disrupting the sexual response cycle through multiple pathways, with sexual anxiety causing impaired sexual functioning, avoidance of intimacy, and loss of sexual pleasure while generating substantial psychological distress. Sexual performance anxiety—characterized by concerns about performance expectations, evaluation, and consequences—stems from dispositional factors including low self-efficacy, negative thought patterns, maladaptive sexual schemas, and personality traits like negative affectivity and introversion, while situational triggers include new sexual encounters and strong self-focus during partnered sex. Physiologically, performance anxiety activates sympathetic nervous system responses that produce vasoconstrictive effects in genital tissues, directly inhibiting erectile response and vaginal lubrication, which then precipitate negative emotions (guilt, shame, embarrassment) that exacerbate worry about future sexual situations and promote avoidance behaviors. The relationship between anxiety and sexual performance follows a nonlinear curve where low-to-moderate anxiety may enhance performance through increased arousal, but high anxiety levels cause deterioration through cognitive distraction and impaired attention to sexual cues. Sexual performance anxiety substantially impacts relationship dynamics, correlating with lower sexual and relationship satisfaction, and can co-occur with broader psychopathology including trait anxiety, internalizing disorders, and social anxiety. These integrated findings underscore the need for multifaceted interventions addressing both direct anxiety reduction and underlying cognitive, emotional, and relational risk factors.

title: Trajectories of menstrual symptoms and blood pressure in midlife: a prospective cohort study on Australian women
summary: This prospective cohort study of Australian women reveals significant associations between menstrual symptom trajectories and cardiovascular health during midlife. Women experiencing increasing heavy menstrual bleeding or chronic irregular periods demonstrated notably elevated diastolic blood pressure (DBP) compared to reference groups, with differences of 3.3 mmHg and 2.5 mmHg respectively. The study proposes that menstrual symptoms may influence long-term blood pressure through endothelial dysfunction, chronic inflammation, and activation of the renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system. Heavy menstrual bleeding showed a bidirectional relationship with chronic hypertension and increased risk of incident hypertension, while irregular periods were associated with related cardiovascular abnormalities, potentially mediated by underlying conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome. Given that even modest DBP elevations substantially increase coronary heart disease risk, particularly in women under age 50, these findings suggest that menstrual symptom patterns warrant consideration as potential cardiovascular health indicators during the midlife transition.

title: Factors Influencing Fertility Distress in Reproductive-Aged Gynecologic Cancer Patients: A Narrative Overview
summary:
# Summary

Gynecological cancers in reproductive-aged women, particularly endometrial and epithelial ovarian cancers, generate substantial fertility distress through multiple pathways: direct damage to reproductive organs and function, combined with treatment-related physiological impairment from surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy. Chemotherapeutic agents such as cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin suppress gonadal function and increase premature menopause risk, while psychological factors—including anxiety, depression, anticipatory grief, and body image changes—amplify distress and significantly influence treatment adherence and quality of life. Sociocultural factors including familial expectations, cultural norms, and support systems substantially shape reproductive decision-making and psychological adjustment, with strong marital intimacy and family support reducing fertility-related anxiety while partner conflict intensifies it. Socioeconomic disparities critically impact outcomes, as financial barriers and limited healthcare access constrain access to fertility preservation options and essential reproductive health services. Multidisciplinary interventions combining fertility counseling, psychological support, and timely educational initiatives effectively mitigate fertility distress, while age-specific assessments are imperative given age's influence on both fertility preservation strategies and associated reproductive conditions such as endometriosis.

title: Understanding the menstrual health self-care practices and experiences among women with physical disabilities in rural Nepal: A qualitative study
summary:
# Summary

Women with physical disabilities in rural Nepal face significant intersecting challenges in menstrual health management, compounded by multifaceted stigma, limited self-awareness, and dependency on family members for medication and hygiene support. Unhygienic menstrual practices associated with inadequate resources and accessibility constraints increase the risk of reproductive tract infections and pelvic inflammatory diseases among this population. While women employ diverse self-care strategies—including homemade remedies (tea with ghee, warm honey water, self-massage) and varied menstrual products (homemade pads in rural areas, commercial pads and menstrual underwear in urban settings)—rural women face particular barriers in accessing appropriate products and disposing of them safely. Female community health volunteers and digital platforms emerge as key interventions for disseminating menstrual hygiene knowledge and promoting proper pad disposal practices. The study emphasizes that disability-inclusive menstrual health information, coupled with improved water and sanitation facilities, diverse product access, and community-based behavior change initiatives, are essential for reducing sexual and reproductive morbidity and improving quality of life among women with disabilities.

title: Robust Bayesian Model Averaging Meta‐Analysis of Menstrual Disorders in COVID‐19 Survivors: A Methodological Meta‐Analysis Study
summary:
# Summary

This meta-analysis examined menstrual disorders in women of reproductive age following SARS-CoV-2 infection during the COVID-19 pandemic, revealing substantial reproductive health impacts. SARS-CoV-2 infection and associated pandemic stressors caused multiple menstrual disturbances, including amenorrhea (12% prevalence), intermenstrual bleeding (17%), regularity changes (24%), duration changes (15%), volume changes (12%), and pain-related changes (17%). The pathophysiology appears multifactorial, involving immune system overreaction, psychological factors such as stress and anxiety, sleep disruption, and vaccination side effects. Severe COVID-19 infection and unvaccinated status were associated with higher prevalence of menstrual disorders. These menstrual disruptions substantially reduced quality of life by impairing physical health and social functioning in affected women, highlighting the significant reproductive consequences of the pandemic on women's health.

title: Multimodal large language models for women’s reproductive mental health
summary:
# Summary

Women's mental health is profoundly influenced by reproductive transitions and associated hormonal fluctuations throughout the lifespan, with conditions such as premenstrual dysphoric disorder, postpartum depression, and menopause-related mood disturbances affecting significant portions of the population. Endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome are moderate-to-high risk factors for mental health conditions, while pregnancy itself presents complex psychiatric challenges including depression, anxiety, and eating disorder relapse, further complicated by social determinants such as financial stress, intimate partner violence, and food insecurity. Untreated mental health conditions during pregnancy carry serious consequences, including increased risk of cesarean delivery and preterm birth, whereas medication discontinuation significantly elevates relapse risk. Treatment approaches including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors and cognitive behavioral therapy effectively manage many reproductive psychiatry conditions, particularly PMDD and perinatal anxiety. The article emphasizes that multimodal large language models represent an emerging opportunity to integrate diverse data streams—including biomarkers like cortisol and estradiol—to provide personalized, timely mental health interventions across women's reproductive lifespan.

title: Longitudinal analysis of an actor-partner interdependence model of recurrent fear and depressive symptoms in elderly patients and caregivers after postoperative adjacent vertebral refracture: a prospective longitudinal study in China
summary: This longitudinal study examined how fear of disease recurrence and depressive symptoms interact bidirectionally in elderly patients experiencing postoperative adjacent vertebral refractures (OVCF) and their spouses. Fear of disease recurrence significantly predicted subsequent depressive symptoms in both patients and spouses, while depressive symptoms reciprocally predicted increased fear of recurrence, establishing a cyclical emotional burden. The study revealed emotional contagion effects, where patients' depressive symptoms positively predicted spouses' depressive symptoms and vice versa, demonstrating that psychological distress extends beyond the patient to affect caregivers. Adjacent vertebral refractures, which commonly occur after percutaneous kyphoplasty (PKP) due to persistent bone loss and increased hardness of treated vertebrae, triggered substantial fear of disease recurrence and associated anxiety and self-doubt. These findings underscore the importance of addressing both patients' and caregivers' psychological well-being through integrated interventions targeting the bidirectional relationship between fear and depression in this population.

title: The relationship between psychological flexibility and health locus of control in patients after total hysterectomy: a latent class analysis
summary: This study examined psychological flexibility and health locus of control in 214 patients following total hysterectomy for conditions including endometriosis, uterine fibroids, and malignancies. Using latent class analysis, researchers identified three distinct patient classes: an Inflexible Class (10.75%) with low psychological flexibility, a Moderate Class (67.29%) with moderate flexibility, and a Flexible Class (21.96%) with high flexibility. Total hysterectomy resulted in significant physiological and psychological complications, including pelvic floor dysfunction, ovarian insufficiency, reduced sexual function, and elevated rates of depression (45.5%), anxiety (68.1%), stress (85.6%), and post-traumatic stress disorder (16.4%). Psychological flexibility emerged as a protective factor, reducing symptoms of stress, anxiety, and depression across patient populations. Demographic factors including younger age (<45 years), higher household income (≥5,000 CNY), and parenthood were associated with different psychological flexibility profiles, while both internal and chance health locus of control showed significant differences across the psychological flexibility classes, suggesting that post-hysterectomy adjustment involves complex interactions between coping mechanisms, personal agency beliefs, and socioeconomic factors.

title: The experiences of transgender and nonbinary individuals in general practice in Denmark, with a focus on ‘safer space’
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Primary Health Care
summary:
# Summary

Transgender and nonbinary individuals in Denmark experience significant health inequities, reporting substantially elevated rates of anxiety, depression, stress, and loneliness alongside widespread unmet healthcare needs and avoidance of primary care services. Negative experiences in general practice—including discrimination, misgendering, physician lack of knowledge about transgender care, and cisnormative assumptions about physiology—create feelings of unsafety that directly discourage healthcare-seeking and disclosure of gender identity issues. Conversely, establishing "safer spaces" through physician behaviors such as using chosen names and pronouns, actively seeking knowledge about transgender care, listening without judgment, and being transparent about limitations significantly improves patient trust and willingness to access care. Healthcare providers who apologize for mistakes, ask clinically relevant questions with clear explanations, and demonstrate genuine interest in meeting patients on their own terms substantially reduce health inequities and support improved mental health outcomes by mitigating stigma and minority stress. The study underscores that cultural safety and affirming physician attitudes are foundational to addressing the documented disparities in mental health and healthcare access experienced by transgender and nonbinary populations.

title: Characteristics and health factors influencing menstrual changes after COVID-19 vaccination: A Spanish retrospective observational study in currently menstruating women
summary:
# Summary

This Spanish retrospective study identified that COVID-19 vaccination causes menstrual changes in approximately 45-51% of menstruating women, with effects intensifying after the second dose. Key risk factors for experiencing menstrual alterations include pre-existing endometriosis, heavy baseline menstrual flow, use of short- to medium-term contraception, higher body mass index, age, and previous pregnancies. Women with pre-existing gynecological conditions, rheumatic disorders, neurological/mental disorders, and those experiencing systemic vaccine adverse events like fatigue, fever, and headache showed significantly higher rates of menstrual disruption. The underlying mechanism likely involves neuro-immune-endocrine interactions affecting the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis, with the timing of vaccination relative to menstrual cycle phase and severity of prior SARS-CoV-2 infection also playing roles. These findings suggest that menstrual changes should be recognized as a documented adverse event of COVID-19 vaccination, particularly in women with pre-existing gynecological or immunological conditions.

title: Sexual Dysfunction after Colorectal Surgery
journal: Clinics in Colon and Rectal Surgery
summary:
# Sexual Dysfunction after Colorectal Surgery

Colorectal surgery frequently causes sexual dysfunction through multiple mechanisms, with autonomic nerve damage being a primary pathway. Surgical trauma, excessive tissue dissection, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy all damage the sympathetic and parasympathetic nerve fibers critical for sexual function, leading to specific dysfunctions: sympathetic nerve injury causes retrograde ejaculation in men, while parasympathetic injury results in erectile dysfunction and decreased vaginal lubrication. Beyond direct nerve damage, inflammation, ischemia, and fibrosis contribute to impaired nerve function, with deeper pelvic dissection and abdominoperineal resection carrying the highest risk. Preexisting sexual dysfunction, older age, advanced tumor stage, and adjuvant therapies represent significant risk factors for postoperative sexual dysfunction. Treatment approaches address both physiological and psychological components, including phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors for erectile dysfunction, psychotherapy and cognitive behavioral therapy for libido and self-image concerns, and hormone replacement therapy for hypogonadism-related symptoms. For colorectal endometriosis specifically, surgical resection can improve fertility outcomes, though laparoscopic approaches generally preserve reproductive function better than open surgery.

title: The lasting impacts of relative energy deficiency in sport imposed on pregnancy health outcomes: A survey-based investigation
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
summary:
# Summary

This survey-based investigation reveals that relative energy deficiency in sport (REDs) prior to conception substantially increases adverse pregnancy and neonatal outcomes. Athletes with a history of REDs demonstrated 3.5 times higher odds of premature labor and 2.6 times higher odds of preterm delivery, with affected infants exhibiting significantly lower birth weights. The mechanism underlying these complications involves REDs-induced suppression of gonadotropin-releasing hormone and reduced reproductive hormones, particularly progesterone, which normally prevents preterm delivery. Secondary amenorrhea associated with low energy availability correlates with increased maternal anemia risk and postpartum menstrual irregularities. Preterm delivery consequences extend beyond the neonatal period, with documented associations to respiratory distress syndrome, sudden infant death syndrome, and long-term complications including cardiovascular disease, kidney disease, and stroke in offspring, while mothers face elevated risks of postpartum depression and cardiovascular complications. These findings underscore the lasting physiological consequences of energy deficiency in athletes and highlight the critical need for nutritional rehabilitation prior to conception to protect both maternal and fetal health outcomes.

title: Estimating age of menopause in mothers in the ALSPAC Study: A data note
summary: This data note from the ALSPAC Study describes methods for estimating age at menopause in a large maternal cohort by analyzing menstrual bleeding patterns while accounting for factors that confound menopause assessment. Earlier menopause is associated with increased risks of multiple chronic conditions including type 2 diabetes, osteoporosis, heart disease, depression, and increased mortality, whereas later age at menopause is linked to increased life expectancy and reduced cardiovascular disease and osteoporosis risk. However, later menopause carries a counterbalancing increase in risk for hormone-sensitive cancers including breast, endometrial, and ovarian cancers. The authors highlight critical methodological considerations for menopause age estimation, including exclusion of data affected by hormonal medications, reproductive surgeries (hysterectomy, oophorectomy), and cancer treatments (chemotherapy, radiation therapy), as well as reproductive events (pregnancy, breastfeeding) that interrupt normal menstrual patterns. This careful approach to data quality ensures accurate menopause age estimates essential for investigating associations between menopausal timing and long-term health outcomes.

title: The effects of nursing interventions on anxiety and depression in infertile females undergoing assisted reproduction: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that diverse nursing interventions significantly reduce anxiety and depression in infertile women undergoing assisted reproductive technology (ART), with mind-body therapies, acupuncture-based approaches, and psychological interventions showing particularly strong effects. Women receiving ART treatment experience substantial psychological distress, as infertility itself is associated with elevated anxiety and depression, with IVF and ovarian stimulation procedures identified as particularly anxiety-provoking. The evidence reveals that interventions implemented before ART treatment are more effective than those administered during or after the procedure, suggesting preventive psychological support optimizes mental health outcomes. Multiple intervention modalities proved effective, including mindfulness-based programs, Hatha yoga, stress management programs, cognitive behavioral therapy, music therapy, acupuncture, and targeted nursing care programs, all demonstrating significant reductions in both anxiety and depression scores compared to control groups. The review emphasizes the clinical importance of psychological support, as psychological stress is associated with decreased pregnancy rates during fertility treatment. These findings highlight nursing's critical role in addressing the psychological burden of infertility and improving mental health outcomes through evidence-based interventions tailored before fertility treatments commence.

title: Psychosocial Resources and Emotional Support Needs in Women with Vulvodynia: A Lifespan Developmental and Biopsychosocial Perspective
summary: Vulvodynia is a chronic vulvar pain condition with multifactorial etiology involving peripheral and central sensitization, inflammatory and immunological mechanisms, pelvic floor hyperactivity, hormonal influences, and stress. The condition significantly impairs women's psychological well-being and quality of life through multiple pathways: chronic pain directly reduces psychological well-being while simultaneously eroding self-efficacy, fostering social withdrawal, and disrupting intimate relationships and sexual satisfaction. Psychological factors including emotional distress, pain-related cognitions, and interpersonal dynamics substantially modulate the pain experience, while greater pain intensity, longer symptom duration, and delayed diagnosis are associated with heightened stress and diminished well-being, particularly in younger women. Critically, psychological well-being correlates strongly with protective factors including social support, dyadic adjustment, psychological resources, and treatment satisfaction, suggesting these domains are essential intervention targets. Psychosocial and educational support interventions effectively reduce emotional distress, while multidisciplinary therapeutic approaches that address physical, psychological, and relational dimensions show promise for managing vulvodynia. Key barriers to effective management include stigma, relationship strain, and fragmented care pathways, highlighting the need for integrated biopsychosocial approaches and early intervention to prevent developmental stagnation and psychological deterioration.

title: Conceptual Frameworks Linking Sexual Health to Physical, Mental, and Interpersonal Well-Being: A Comprehensive Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary: This comprehensive systematic review and meta-analysis establishes sexual health as a multidimensional construct with robust associations across physical, mental, and interpersonal domains. Key dimensions of sexual health—including sexual satisfaction, desire, orgasmic functioning, absence of sexual pain, and adequate vaginal lubrication—demonstrate consistent bidirectional relationships with psychological well-being, reduced psychological distress, enhanced relationship satisfaction, and improved physical health outcomes. Orgasmic frequency emerged as a particularly strong predictor, correlating with elevated self-esteem, better life coping skills, reduced subjective stress, and greater relationship investment and quality. Conversely, sexual dysfunction, particularly orgasm difficulties, was significantly associated with depression, attachment anxiety, and lower overall satisfaction, with iatrogenic effects observed from antidepressant medications that impair orgasmic function. The analysis reveals that sexual health inversely correlates with attachment anxiety and avoidance, suggesting that secure romantic relationships and adequate sexual function are interdependent. These findings underscore sexual health as an integral component of holistic well-being, with implications for treating conditions like endometriosis that compromise multiple dimensions of sexual function simultaneously.

title: Exploring Adolescents' Menstrual Hygiene Management Needs: Findings From Students in St. Louis, Missouri Charter Schools
journal: Perspectives on Sexual and Reproductive Health
summary: This study examines menstrual hygiene management challenges among adolescent students in St. Louis charter schools, revealing that nearly 69% of respondents missed at least one school day per month due to menstruation-related issues, with menstrual pain (62%), heavy bleeding (55%), inadequate period products (41%), and odor (43%) as primary contributors. Period poverty significantly impacts educational outcomes, as 51% of students reported inability to afford menstrual products and 41% avoided using school restrooms due to privacy concerns or unsanitary conditions. While 97% of students track their periods and 80% use disposable pads, over one-third experienced school discipline problems related to menstruation, and students reported needing access to diverse product types, over-the-counter pain medications, and heating pads to manage symptoms effectively during the school day. Beyond physical symptoms, menstruation is associated with emotional burden including self-consciousness, embarrassment, and premenstrual emotional changes that compound educational disruption. The study identifies a critical gap in menstrual health education, with only 3% of students citing school as their primary information source, compared to 60% learning from family and friends. These findings underscore the urgent need for schools to provide comprehensive period product access, diverse product options, symptom management resources, and evidence-based menstrual health education to support student well-being and academic engagement.

title: Risk factors of decisional conflict among people living with chronic pain identified through a pan-Canadian survey
summary:
# Summary

Among people living with chronic pain, clinically significant decisional conflict—a psychological state arising from difficulty making healthcare decisions—is driven by multiple intrapersonal, interpersonal, and systemic factors. Key risk factors include low health literacy, limited knowledge of treatment options, poor communication with clinicians, unclear personal values, and psychosocial distress, with specific pain types such as pelvic and low limb pain showing increased association with decisional conflict. Conversely, protective factors include higher decision self-efficacy, quality of life, role congruence between assumed and preferred decision-making roles, and collaborative interactions with healthcare providers where patient preferences align with chosen treatment options. Decisional conflict substantially increases the likelihood of delayed decisions and regret, underscoring the clinical importance of interventions such as shared decision-making, decision coaching, and patient decision aids in reducing conflict and improving treatment outcomes. Education on available options prior to clinical consultations, combined with collaborative decision-making approaches that respect patient preferences and reduce consultation-related stress, represents an evidence-based strategy for minimizing decisional conflict in chronic pain populations.

title: Protective factors against depression: the role of personality, spiritual fitness, and positive mental health in a two-wave longitudinal study of Polish adults
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal study of Polish adults identified multiple protective factors against depression, with positive mental health emerging as the strongest predictor of reduced depressive symptoms. Among personality dimensions, emotional stability, conscientiousness, and extraversion each independently reduced depression risk, with emotional stability showing the largest effect among personality traits. Spiritual fitness components also played protective roles, particularly the active pursuit of meaning, purpose, and values, which buffered against depression by facilitating existential meaning-making and resilience. Notably, positive mental health mediated the protective effects of personality traits on depression outcomes, suggesting it represents a key mechanism through which stable, conscientious, and extraverted individuals maintain mental health. Interestingly, personal connection to a higher power showed an unexpected small positive association with depressive symptoms, possibly reflecting spiritual struggles or crisis of faith. Overall, the study demonstrates that depression prevention benefits from a multifaceted approach integrating personality strengths, psychological well-being resources, and spiritual dimensions of meaning and purpose.

title: Is exercise duration more important than intensity in endometriosis?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent, inflammatory condition characterized by defective immune function that allows endometrial tissue to implant in the pelvic peritoneum, ovaries, fallopian tubes, and in rare cases, extrapelvic sites. Elevated estradiol levels perpetuate a pro-inflammatory environment and promote endometrial growth, while the resulting widespread inflammation correlates with increased symptom severity and reduced quality of life. Exercise duration per individual session emerged as a key protective factor, with longer exercise sessions inversely associated with inflammatory markers (CRP), estradiol levels, both android and gynoid fat mass, and endometriosis-related pain. Additionally, increased pain and poor body fat distribution correlated with psychological distress including depression, anxiety, poor self-image, and reduced social support, whereas exercise-induced elevation in cortisol and reduction in inflammatory biomarkers suggest a mechanistic pathway through which exercise alleviates both physical symptoms and mental health outcomes in women with endometriosis.

title: ‘Trust me, I’m a Doctor’: examining the influence of threat perceptions, stigma, emotion, and distrust on reproductive health data falsification
summary: This study examined psychological and social factors driving reproductive health data falsification among patients. The research identifies a cascade of influences wherein perceived threat (susceptibility and severity), stigma, and negative emotions foster distrust in health professionals, which in turn strongly predicts intentions to falsify reproductive health data. Notably, perceived control over one's health situation acts as a protective factor, reducing both distrust and falsification intentions. The consequences of this data falsification are substantial: inaccurate information provided to healthcare providers compromises diagnosis quality, care access, and patient outcomes. Demographic factors also play roles, with older age and certain political affiliations associated with lower falsification intentions, while prior abortion experience and higher household income correlate with increased falsification. These findings underscore how stigma-driven distrust creates a vicious cycle where vulnerable reproductive health patients strategically withhold or alter sensitive health information, ultimately undermining the clinical care they seek.

title: Overlooked and undertreated: gendered ageism in primary care management of eating disorders
journal: International Journal for Equity in Health
summary: Eating disorders are substantially underrecognized in primary care due to gendered ageism, with healthcare professionals exhibiting limited awareness that these conditions persist and can re-emerge across the lifespan beyond the stereotypical presentation in young, affluent women. While eating disorders classically present with weight loss, appetite changes, and disrupted eating patterns, symptoms in older adults are frequently misattributed to normal age-related changes, leading to delayed diagnosis and treatment. Late-life eating disorders are often triggered by major psychosocial transitions including retirement, bereavement, caregiving responsibilities, and declining health, and are further complicated by biological factors such as hormonal shifts during menopause and testosterone decline. These disorders frequently co-occur with depression, anxiety, and obsessive-compulsive personality disorders, yet body dissatisfaction remains a stable risk factor across all age groups. Successful treatment outcomes require a multidimensional approach combining hospitalization, psychotherapy, pharmacological interventions, and behavioral interventions, with emerging evidence supporting electroconvulsive therapy in select cases. The article underscores that clinical recognition of eating disorders in aging populations is essential to prevent prolonged undertreatment of these serious psychiatric conditions.

title: Psychological distress predicts disease activity in inflammatory bowel disease: Results from the mind-body IBD longitudinal study
summary:
# Summary

This longitudinal study demonstrates that psychological distress—encompassing depression, anxiety, and chronic stress—is a significant predictor of disease activity and healthcare burden in inflammatory bowel disease, independent of objective inflammatory markers. Baseline psychological distress predicted higher self-reported disease activity six months later, with impaired sleep quality mediating approximately 55% of this effect. The study reveals that psychological distress independently predicts multiple adverse outcomes including disease flares, absenteeism, productivity loss, and increased secondary healthcare utilization, operating through multiple pathways including altered gut-brain signaling, somatic hypervigilance, and health anxiety. Importantly, psychological factors correlate more strongly with patient-reported symptom measures than with objective inflammation, suggesting that untreated distress can inflate symptom scores and increase healthcare burden regardless of actual inflammatory status. The findings underscore that psychological interventions targeting stress, sleep quality, and health behaviors may represent a critical complement to biomedical treatments for reducing both disease activity and the economic costs of IBD.

title: No anxiety or pain reduction by Virtual Reality during oocyte retrieval in IVF/ICSI treatment: results of a randomized controlled trial
summary:
# Summary

Despite previous evidence that virtual reality successfully reduces anxiety and pain during various gynecological procedures, this randomized controlled trial found no significant differences in pre- or post-procedural anxiety, overall pain, or peak pain between patients receiving VR during oocyte retrieval and control participants. Oocyte retrieval is inherently anxiety-provoking and painful due to needle passage through vaginal tissue and the ovarian capsule, and while VR provided moderate to high distraction and immersion, these cognitive benefits did not translate into measurable reductions in procedural anxiety or pain. Interestingly, participants who received VR reported significantly fewer adverse sensory symptoms including dizziness, headache, and blurred vision compared to controls, and demonstrated greater willingness to undergo future retrievals with VR and higher likelihood of recommending it to others, suggesting perceived psychological benefit despite lack of objective anxiety or pain reduction. These findings suggest that VR may be less effective for oocyte retrieval than for other gynecological procedures, possibly due to the intensity of the procedural stimulus, and highlight the distinction between subjective patient preference and objective clinical outcomes in fertility treatment.

title: ‘They can induce and exacerbate each other’ – the complex interplay between domestic abuse and the perimenopause: a qualitative study with female survivors
summary: This qualitative study reveals a complex bidirectional relationship between domestic abuse and perimenopause, where each condition intensifies the other in female survivors. Domestic abuse directly causes anxiety, depression, PTSD, mood changes, and sleep disturbances, while perimenopause generates its own constellation of physical and psychological symptoms including hot flushes, night sweats, joint aches, fatigue, brain fog, and cognitive difficulties. Partner violence specifically increases the risk of early menopause and amplifies menopausal symptoms such as vasomotor disturbances and vaginal issues, with effects particularly pronounced in women with complex post-traumatic stress disorder. The study identifies a previously unreported mechanism in which abusive partners weaponize perimenopausal symptoms as a form of coercive control, complicating clinical assessment by creating symptom overlap between trauma responses and menopausal manifestations. Women with comorbid conditions including endometriosis, fibromyalgia, and PCOS experience heightened symptom complexity during perimenopause. Notably, some participants reported that perimenopause paradoxically provided emotional clarity that enabled them to recognize abuse and make decisions to leave.

title: Association between body image satisfaction and depressive symptoms among young women in the UAE with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (PCOS) and Non-PCOS: A comparative quantitative cross-sectional study
summary: This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between body image satisfaction and depressive symptoms in young women with and without PCOS in the UAE, revealing that women with PCOS experience significantly elevated depressive symptoms and body image concerns compared to non-PCOS controls. PCOS produces multiple physical manifestations—including hirsutism, scalp hair loss, acne, irregular periods, obesity, and elevated androgen levels—that collectively contribute to negative body image perceptions and psychological distress encompassing depression, anxiety, and sleep disturbances. Higher body mass index correlated strongly with reduced body satisfaction but showed no significant association with depressive symptom severity. Comprehensive management strategies combining weight management and hormonal treatments were identified as effective approaches to improve both mental and physical health outcomes in this population. The findings underscore that PCOS-related psychological morbidity is driven by the cumulative impact of visible physical symptoms on body image, suggesting that addressing both somatic and psychological dimensions is essential for improving quality of life in affected women.

title: A Roadmap for Advancing Women's Health in Disorders of the Gut‐Brain Interaction
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the intricate relationship between sex hormones, the menstrual cycle, and gastrointestinal symptoms in women's health disorders. Fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone throughout the menstrual cycle drive changes in colonic motility through prostaglandin signaling (particularly PGE2 and PGF2α), resulting in constipation during the luteal phase and softer stools at menstruation, with heightened visceral sensitivity and pain during menses. Women with endometriosis experience remarkably high rates of chronic GI symptoms (up to 90%) including constipation, bloating, and abdominal pain, with endometriosis conferring a threefold increased risk of irritable bowel syndrome; this association may be mediated through intestinal microbiome dysbiosis, which influences estrogen metabolism. The article identifies multiple interconnected gynecological and systemic conditions that compound GI dysfunction, including polycystic ovary syndrome and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, which show extraordinarily high rates of concurrent GI disorders. Hormonal interventions such as oral contraceptive pills and emerging pharmacologic therapies like linaclotide show promise for symptom relief, while dietary modifications such as low FODMAP diets demonstrate clinical benefit in patients with overlapping endometriosis and IBS.

title: Communications – I International Congress of the Scientific Society of Psychoneuroimmunology (SOCIPNI), Granada, November 13–14, 2025
summary: This psychoneuroimmunological study reveals bidirectional relationships between psychological stress, trauma, immune dysregulation, and mental health outcomes. Perinatal anxiety, chronic stress, and elevated hair cortisol levels are strongly associated with psychopathological symptoms and depression, while post-traumatic stress disorder shows elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ), demonstrating how psychological trauma triggers persistent systemic inflammation. Dietary factors significantly modulate these pathways: ultra-processed food consumption correlates with depressive symptoms, elevated inflammatory markers, and altered brain morphology in the anterior cingulate cortex, while whole grains and legume consumption associate with improved quality of life. The microbiota emerges as a critical mediator, with specific bacterial taxa (Coprococcus catus, Desulfovibrio piger) linked to depression and PTSD, whereas beneficial bacteria (Bifidobacterium, Gemmiger, Ruminococcus) support mental health. Buchinger fasting represents a novel intervention reducing oxidative stress while enhancing mucosal immunity through autophagy activation. Finally, intimate partner violence survivors exhibit dysregulated cortisol, reduced salivary immunoglobulin A, and heightened anxiety alongside altered neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios, highlighting how psychosocial trauma leaves lasting immunological imprints.

title: Long-term changes in health-related quality of life among Australian adults with psychological distress: a 16-year perspective
summary: This 16-year longitudinal study of Australian adults demonstrates that psychological distress significantly and persistently reduces health-related quality of life across multiple domains, with effects intensifying at higher severity levels. Severe psychological distress reduces health state utilities by up to 0.177 points and can decrease life expectancy by 20 years through both physical illness and suicide, while even mild psychological distress produces measurable declines. The impact of psychological distress on quality of life is particularly pronounced in mental health and physical functioning domains, with disproportionate effects on role-related activities. Sociodemographic factors including education, employment, physical activity, and higher income were associated with better quality of life, whereas smoking and elevated body mass index worsened outcomes. Age and sex differences emerged, with older adults and women experiencing more substantial quality of life declines related to psychological distress. The findings underscore that psychological distress—particularly depression and anxiety—triggers cascading chronic disease burden and emphasize the importance of addressing mental health as a modifiable determinant of long-term health outcomes and life expectancy in the general population.

title: Impact of Vulvodynia on the Quality of Life of Women: A Rapid Review
summary: Vulvodynia, characterized by chronic vulvar pain and hypersensitivity, substantially impairs women's quality of life across physical, psychological, and social dimensions. The condition causes dyspareunia and sexual dysfunction while triggering significant psychological comorbidities including anxiety, depression, stress, and emotional distress; notably, women with vulvodynia experience greater psychological distress than those with other chronic conditions. Cognitive factors such as catastrophizing, hypervigilance, and fear of pain amplify symptom severity through a bidirectional relationship between emotional experience and pain perception, while behavioral avoidance, fatigue, and physical limitations restrict daily activities and social participation. Effective management requires multimodal approaches combining physiotherapy, psychological therapy, and mindfulness-based interventions; these strategies improve pain control, emotional well-being, and anxiety reduction, though complete resolution remains challenging. Relationship factors including partner empathy, open communication, and family support serve as protective elements for emotional adjustment and social engagement. Future clinical approaches must address the complex biopsychosocial nature of vulvodynia through integrated interventions targeting both symptom management and psychological adjustment.

title: Perspectives on gender-inclusive language in Canadian sexual and reproductive health care: findings from a modified Delphi survey
summary:
# Summary

This modified Delphi survey reveals that gender-inclusive language significantly improves sexual and reproductive healthcare experiences for Two-Spirit, transgender, and nonbinary (2STGNB) individuals in Canada, while exclusionary and cisnormative practices create substantial barriers to care. Misgendering and exclusionary healthcare practices increase psychological distress, postpartum depression, and care avoidance, while contributing to poorer health outcomes including undertreatment of gynecological conditions, lower cervical cancer screening rates, and higher risks of perinatal depression and anxiety. The study demonstrates that clinician competency is directly associated with affirming practices, including introducing personal pronouns and asking patients for theirs, suggesting that targeted training could improve care quality. Systemic discrimination in sexual and reproductive health settings actively impedes 2STGNB individuals' timely access to care, underscoring the need for healthcare systems to adopt gender-inclusive language and practices to address documented disparities in sexual and reproductive health outcomes for this population.

title: Exploring the Experiences and Reproductive Health Needs of Young Women With Premature Ovarian Insufficiency: A Qualitative Content Analysis
summary: Premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is characterized by loss of ovarian function before age 40, resulting in hypoestrogenism, amenorrhea, and infertility. The estrogen deficiency associated with POI causes significant sexual dysfunction, including vaginal dryness, loss of elasticity, and dyspareunia, while the condition also elevates long-term risks for cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, cognitive decline, and diabetes. Women with POI experience substantial psychological burden, including anxiety, depression, and marital strain, particularly when infertility contributes to relationship discord. Hormone replacement therapy effectively treats hypoestrogenism and mitigates both symptomatic and long-term health consequences. The study emphasizes that comprehensive management requires multidisciplinary, patient-centered care alongside culturally tailored psychological support and support groups to address the complex reproductive health and mental health needs of affected women.

title: Hand Eczema and Self‐Reported Work Ability in Middle‐Aged Cohort: A Northern Finland Birth Cohort 1966 Study
summary: Hand eczema is a common inflammatory skin condition affecting the hands and wrists that causes itching, pain, fissures, vesicles, and scaling, with significantly higher prevalence in females (15.6%) compared to males (9.5%). The physical symptoms and visible skin damage impair work ability both directly—through discomfort that makes manual tasks difficult—and indirectly, by triggering psychological distress including shame and feelings of self-consciousness that affect occupational functioning. In a Northern Finland birth cohort study, individuals with hand eczema had substantially elevated risk of reporting poor work ability, with consequences including increased sick leave and job changes. The condition is associated with comorbid factors including obesity, low physical activity, and musculoskeletal disorders, which may compound workplace difficulties. Poor work ability scores in hand eczema patients predict long-term and permanent work disability, as well as mental and physical work strain in midlife and disability after retirement, indicating that hand eczema represents a significant occupational health concern requiring clinical attention.

title: Experience of endometriosis pain: a qualitative study
summary:
# Summary

This qualitative study reveals that endometriosis causes multifaceted impacts on women's wellbeing beyond pelvic pain, including fatigue, brain fog, bloating, and compromised quality of life across physical, emotional, and social domains. The pain is typically unpredictable and cyclically related to menstruation and ovulation, with variable severity that undermines patients' sense of control and creates difficulty planning daily activities. Psychologically, endometriosis pain triggers anxiety, depression, hopelessness, and even suicidal ideation in some patients, while also generating isolation, negative body image, and strain in relationships due to others minimizing their experience. The unpredictable nature of pain drives catastrophic thinking and fear-based avoidance behaviors that further restrict normal activities, employment, and education, perpetuating disability through fear and avoidance models similar to other chronic pain conditions. Notably, surgical and hormonal treatments provide limited efficacy and may cause additional problems, leaving many women inadequately treated. The study emphasizes that endometriosis's psychological burden, social disruption, and fertility concerns demand comprehensive pain management approaches that address both the biophysiological aspects—including central sensitization and comorbid pain conditions like fibromyalgia and bladder pain—and the cognitive-behavioral and emotional dimensions of the disease.

title: Stigma experiences among young and middle-aged testicular cancer survivors in China: A phenomenological study
